sections.heading: Abstract
sections.summary: Graph generative models are essential across diverse scientific domains by capturing complex distributions over relational data. Among them, graph diffusion models achieve superior performance but face inefficient sampling and limited flexibility due to the tight coupling between training and sampling stages.

sections.heading: 1. Introduction
sections.summary: Graph generation has become a fundamental task across diverse fields, from molecular chemistry to social network analysis, due to graphs' capacity to represent complex relationships and generate realistic structured data. Diffusion-based graph generative models [\(Niu et al.,](#page-10-0) [2020;](#page-10-0) [Jo et al.,](#page-9-0) [2022\)](#page-9-0), particularly those tailored for discrete data [\(Vignac et al.,](#page-11-0) [2022\)](#page-11-0), have emerged as compelling ap-

sections.heading: 2. Background
sections.summary: In generative modeling, the primary goal is to generate new data samples from the underlying distribution that produced the original data, $p_{\rm data}$ . An effective approach is to learn a mapping between a simpler distribution $p_{\epsilon}$ that can be easily sampled, and $p_{\rm data}$ .

sections.heading: 3. DeFoG Framework
sections.summary: In this section, we present DeFoG (Discrete Flow Matching on Graphs), a novel iterative refinement framework for graph generation that leverages the decoupling of training and sampling stages. We begin by describing its noising and denoising processes, highlighting how they enable this disentanglement, as illustrated in Figure 1b.

sections.heading: 3.1. Learning Discrete Flows over Graphs
sections.summary: We instantiate undirected graphs with N nodes as $G=(x^{1:n:N},e^{1:i< j:N})$ , where $x^{1:n:N}=(x^{(n)})_{1\leq n\leq N}$ and $e^{1:i< j:N}=(e^{(ij)})_{1\leq i< j\leq N}$ denote the node and edge sets, respectively, with $x^{(n)}\in\mathcal{X}=\{1,\ldots,X\}$ and $e^{(ij)}\in\mathcal{E}=\{1,\ldots,E\}$ . Following standard practice in the field (Vignac et al., 2022; Xu et al., 2024; Siraudin et al., 2024), we consider an edge between every pair of nodes, where one of the edge categories explicitly represents the absence of an edge (i.e., a "non-existing" edge).)

sections.summary: 1: Input: Graph dataset $$\mathcal{D} = \{G^1, \dots, G^M\}$$

sections.heading: Algorithm 2 DeFoG Sampling
sections.summary: ``` 1: Input: # graphs to sample S

sections.heading: 3.2. Design Space of DeFoG
sections.summary: As described in the previous section, DeFoG benefits from greater flexibility due to its training-sampling decoupling.

sections.heading: 3.3. Permutation Invariance Guarantees
sections.summary: Lemma 3 (Node Permutation Equivariance and Invariance Properties of DeFoG). *For any permutation equivariant denoising neural network, the loss function of DeFoG is permutation invariant, and its sampling probability is permutation invariant.*

sections.heading: 4. Related Work
sections.summary: Graph Generative Models Graph generation has applications across various domains, including molecular generation [\(Mercado et al.,](#page-10-5) [2021\)](#page-10-5), combinatorial optimization [\(Sun & Yang,](#page-11-1) [2024\)](#page-11-1), and inverse protein folding [\(Yi](#page-11-3) [et al.,](#page-11-3) [2024\)](#page-11-3). Existing methods for this task generally fall into two main categories.

sections.heading: 5. Experiments
sections.summary: This section highlights DeFoG's SOTA performance, enabled by its highly decoupled framework and effective sampling methods. We present DeFoG's performance in generating graphs with diverse topological structures and in molecular datasets with rich prior information.

sections.heading: 5.1. Graph Generation Performance
sections.summary: Synthetic Graph Generation We evaluate DeFoG using the widely adopted *Planar*, *SBM* [\(Martinkus et al.,](#page-10-9) [2022\)](#page-10-9), and *Tree* datasets [\(Bergmeister et al.,](#page-8-8) [2023\)](#page-8-8), along with the associated evaluation methodology. In Tab.

sections.heading: 5.2. Efficiency Improvement
sections.summary: We now show that DeFoG enhances both training and sampling efficiency significantly across diverse datasets.

sections.heading: 5.3. Ablations
sections.summary: Here, we focus on evaluating the impact of different sampling approaches. We start from the vanilla sampling setup and sweep over sample distortion, target guidance, or stochasticity independently.

sections.heading: 6. Conclusion
sections.summary: We introduce DeFoG, a novel discrete flow matching framework for graphs. This formulation enables training-sampling decoupling, which we ground theoretically to ensure faithful graph distribution modeling.

sections.heading: Acknowledgements
sections.summary: We would like to thank Clement Vignac and Andrew ´ Campbell for the useful discussions and suggestions.

sections.heading: Impact Statement
sections.summary: The primary objective of this paper is to advance graph generation under a more flexible framework, with applications spanning general graph generation, molecular design, and digital pathology. The ability to generate graphs with discrete labels can have broad-reaching implications for fields such as drug discovery and diagnostic technologies.

sections.heading: References
sections.summary: - Asthana, R., Conrad, J., Dawoud, Y., Ortmanns, M., and Belagiannis, V. Multi-conditioned graph diffusion for neural architecture search.

sections.heading: Appendix Overview
sections.summary: In the Appendix, we provide additional details organized as follows:

sections.heading: A. Contextualizing Related Research
sections.summary: In this section, we further contextualize DeFoG within the scope of related work. We begin by introducing the methods used for comparison with DeFoG in Appendix [A.1.](#page-13-1) Subsequently, we outline the key distinctions between DeFoG and existing diffusion-based graph generative models in Appendix [A.2.](#page-13-2)

sections.heading: A.1. Overview of Compared Methods
sections.summary: In Sec. [5,](#page-5-0) we evaluate DeFoG against a diverse set of graph generative models, which we introduce below:

sections.heading: A.2. DeFoG and Graph Diffusion Models
sections.summary: In this section, we contextualize DeFoG in relation to existing graph diffusion models.

sections.heading: A.2.1. FROM CONTINUOUS TO DISCRETE STATE-SPACES
sections.summary: Early diffusion-based graph generative models extended continuous diffusion and score-based methods from image generation to graphs by relaxing adjacency matrices into continuous state-spaces [\(Niu et al.,](#page-10-0) [2020;](#page-10-0) [Jo et al.,](#page-9-0) [2022\)](#page-9-0). However, this approach overlooks the inherent discreteness of graph-structured data, resulting in topologically uninformed noising processes.

sections.heading: A.2.2. From Discrete to Continuous time
sections.summary: The initial discrete-time diffusion frameworks for graph generation (Vignac et al., 2022; Haefeli et al., 2022) were built upon Discrete Denoising Diffusion Probabilistic Models (D3PMs) (Austin et al., 2021), which operate with a fixed partitioning of time. This discretization constrains the model to denoise at specific time points and ties the sampling process to the same fixed time steps used during training, leading to a rigid coupling between the training and sampling stages.

sections.heading: A.2.3. From Continuous-Time Discrete Diffusion to Discrete Flow Matching
sections.summary: While both continuous-time discrete diffusion and discrete flow matching (DFM) share the CTMC formulation for the denoising process, they differ fundamentally in the formulation of the noising process. Continuous-time discrete diffusion-based graph generative models (Xu et al., 2024; Siraudin et al., 2024) define the noising process as a CTMC, akin to the denoising process.

sections.heading: A.2.4. MIXED INTEGRATION OF CONTINUOUS AND DISCRETE STATE-SPACES
sections.summary: Integrating continuous and categorical data within graph generative models is an important challenge, as many real-world applications involve heterogeneous data types (e.g., molecular graphs containing atomic coordinates alongside categorical atom and bond types). A recent example addressing this challenge is GBD (Liu et al., 2024), which incorporates beta diffusion to jointly model both continuous and discrete variables.

sections.heading: B. Sample Optimization
sections.summary: In this section, we explore the proposed sampling optimization in more detail. We start by analysing the different time distortion functions in Appendix B.1.

sections.heading: B.1. Time Distortion Functions
sections.summary: In Sec. 3, we explore the utilization of different *distortion functions*, i.e., functions that are used to transform time.

sections.heading: B.2. Target Guidance
sections.summary: In this section, we demonstrate that the proposed *target guidance* design for the rate matrices violates the Kolmogorov equation with an error that is linear in $\omega$ . This result indicates that a small guidance factor effectively helps fit the distribution, whereas a larger guidance factor, as shown in Figure 9, while enhancing topological properties such as planarity, increases the distance between generated and training data on synthetic datasets according to the metrics of average ratio.

sections.heading: B.3. Detailed Balance, Prior Incorporation, and Stochasticity
sections.summary: Campbell et al. (2024) show that although their $z_1$ -conditional formulation of $R_t^*$ generates $p_{t|1}$ , it does not span the full space of valid rate matrices — those that satisfy the conditional Kolmogorov equation (Eq.

sections.heading: B.4. Hyperparameter Optimization Pipeline
sections.summary: A significant advantage of flow matching methods is their inherently greater flexibility in the sampling process compared to diffusion models, as they are more disentangled from the training stage. Each of the proposed optimization strategies exposed in Sec.

sections.heading: B.5. Understanding the Sampling Dynamics of $R_t^*$
sections.summary: In this section, we aim to provide deeper intuition into the sampling dynamics imposed by the design of $R_t^*$ , as proposed by (Campbell et al., 2024). The explicit formulation of $R_t^*$ can be found in Eq.

sections.heading: B.6. Performance Improvement for Undertrained Models
sections.summary: In this section, we present the performance of a model trained on the QM9 dataset and the Planar dataset using only 30% of the epochs compared to the final model being reported. We employ the same hyperparameters as in Tab.

sections.heading: C. Train Optimization
sections.summary: In this section, we provide a more detailed analysis of the influence of the various training optimization strategies introduced in Sec. 3.

sections.heading: C.1. Initial Distributions
sections.summary: Under DeFoG's framework, the noising process for each dimension is modeled as a linear interpolation between the clean data distribution (the one-hot representation of the current state) and an initial distribution, $p_0$ . As such, it is intuitive that different initial distributions result in varying performances, depending on the denoising dynamics they induce.

sections.heading: C.2. Interaction between Sample and Train Distortions
sections.summary: From Appendix B.4, we observe that time distortions applied during the sampling stage can significantly affect performance. This suggests that graph discrete flow models do not behave evenly across time and are more sensitive to specific time intervals, where generative performance benefits from finer updates achieved by using smaller time steps.

sections.heading: D. Theoretical Results
sections.summary: In this section, we provide the proofs of the different theoretical results of the paper. First, we provide results that are domain agnostic, i.e., that hold for general discrete data, and then instantiate them for the specific case of graphs, yielding Theorems 1 and 2.

sections.heading: D.1. Theoretical Results on the Generative Framework
sections.summary: In Appendix D.1.1, we provide a proof of the bounded estimation error of the rate matrix for general discrete data, and instantiate it for graphs, yielding Theorem 1. Then, in Appendix D.1.2, we prove the bounded deviation of the generated distribution for general discrete data as well, and instantiate it for graphs, yielding Theorem 2.

sections.heading: D.1.1. BOUNDED ESTIMATION ERROR OF UNCONDITIONAL MULTIVARIATE RATE MATRIX
sections.summary: We start by introducing two important concepts, which will reveal useful for the proof of the intended result.

sections.heading: D.1.2. BOUNDED DEVIATION OF THE GENERATED DISTRIBUTION
sections.summary: As in Appendix D.1.1, we start by introducing the necessary concepts that will reveal useful for the proof of the intended result.

sections.heading: D.1.3. CRITICAL ANALYSIS AND POSITIONING OF THEOREM 1 AND THEOREM 2
sections.summary: Theorem 1 establishes that minimizing the cross-entropy (CE) loss directly corresponds to minimizing an upper bound on the rate matrix estimation error. This result provides a direct and principled justification for using the CE loss, as it promotes accurate sampling from the underlying CTMC.

sections.heading: D.2. Theoretical Results on Architectural Expressivity
sections.summary: We now proceed to the graph specific theoretical results.

sections.heading: D.2.1. NODE PERMUTATION EQUIVARIANCE AND INVARIANCE PROPERTIES
sections.summary: The different components of a graph generative model have to respect different graph symmetries. For example, the permutation equivariance of the model architecture ensures the output changes consistently with any reordering of input nodes, while permutation-invariant loss evaluates the model's performance consistently across isomorphic graphs, regardless of node order.

sections.heading: D.2.2. ADDITIONAL FEATURES EXPRESSIVITY
sections.summary: This section explains the expressivity of the RRWP features used in DeFoG. We summarize the findings of Ma et al.

sections.heading: E. Conditional Generation
sections.summary: In this section, we describe how to seamlessly integrate DeFoG with existing methods for CTMC-based conditioning mechanisms. In this setting, all the examples are assumed to have a label.

sections.heading: F. Experimental Details
sections.summary: This section provides further details on the experimental settings used in the paper.

sections.heading: F.1. Architecture and Additional Features
sections.summary: **Denoising Neural Architecture** DeFoG's denoising neural network takes a noisy graph $G_t$ as input and predicts the clean marginal probability for each node $x^{(n)}$ via $p_{1|t}^{\theta,(n)}(\cdot|G_t)$ and for each edge $e^{(ij)}$ via $p_{1|t}^{\theta,(ij)}(\cdot|G_t)$ . This formulation boils down the graph generative task to a graph-to-graph mapping.

sections.heading: F.2.1. SYNTHETIC DATASETS
sections.summary: Here, we describe the datasets employed in our experiments and outline the specific metrics used to evaluate model performance on each dataset. Additional visualizations of example graphs from each dataset, along with generated graphs, are provided in Figures 16 to 18.

sections.heading: F.2.2. MOLECULAR DATASETS
sections.summary: **Description** Molecular generation is a key real-world application of graph generation. It poses a challenging task to current graph generation models to their rich chemistry-specific information, involving several nodes and edges classes and leaning how to generate them jointly, and more complex evaluation pipelines.

sections.heading: F.2.3. DIGITAL PATHOLOGY DATASETS
sections.summary: **Description** Graphs, with their natural ability to represent relational data, are widely used to capture spatial biological dependencies in tissue images. This approach has proven successful in digital pathology tasks such as microenvironment classification (Wu et al., 2022), cancer classification (Pati et al., 2022), and decision explainability (Jaume et al., 2020).

sections.heading: F.3. Resources
sections.summary: The training and sampling times for the different datasets explored in this paper are provided in Tab. [4.](#page-48-1) All the experiments in this work were run on a single NVIDIA A100-SXM4-80GB GPU.

sections.heading: F.4. Hyperparameter Tuning
sections.summary: The default hyperparameters for training and sampling for each dataset can be found in the provided code repository. In Tab.

sections.heading: G. Additional Results
sections.summary: First, we discuss conditional generation for real-world digital pathology graph generation. Next, we provide comprehensive tables detailing results on synthetic datasets in Appendix G.2.

sections.heading: G.1. Conditional Generation
sections.summary: Table 6: TLS conditional generation results.

sections.heading: G.2. Synthetic Graph Generation
sections.summary: In Tab. 7, we present the full results for DeFoG for the three different datasets: planar, tree, and SBM.

sections.heading: G.3. Molecular Graph Generation
sections.summary: For the molecular generation tasks, we begin by examining the results for QM9, considering both implicit and explicit hydrogens (Vignac et al., 2022). In the implicit case, hydrogen atoms are inferred to complete the valencies, while in the explicit case, hydrogens must be explicitly modeled, making it an inherently more challenging task.

sections.heading: G.4. Impact of Additional Features
sections.summary: In graph diffusion methods, the task of graph generation is decomposed into a mapping of a graph to a set of marginal probabilities for each node and edge. This problem is typically addressed using a Graph Transformer architecture, which is augmented with additional features to capture structural aspects that the base architecture might struggle to model effectively (Vignac et al., 2022; Xu et al., 2024; Siraudin et al., 2024) otherwise.

entities.name: Divergence Artifacts
entities.description: Unrealistic oversaturated pixels from high-order ODE solvers with few steps or high guidance. Manifest as high latent magnitudes.

entities.name: Stability Region
entities.description: Complex-plane region where ODE solutions converge. If δλ falls outside, solution diverges. Higher-order methods have smaller regions.

entities.name: Heavy Ball Momentum
entities.description: Polyak's method applied to diffusion: v_{n+1}=(1-β)v_n+βΣb_i f(x_{n-i}), x_{n+1}=x_n+δv_{n+1}. Expands stability but drops to first-order.

entities.name: Generalized Heavy Ball (GHVB)
entities.description: High-order momentum: applies filter (β+(1-β)Δ) before summing high-order terms. Momentum number ceiling = method order. Interpolates adjacent AB methods.

entities.name: Damping Coefficient (β)
entities.description: Parameter in (0,1] trading accuracy vs artifact suppression. β=1 recovers original; smaller β enlarges stability region.

entities.name: Adams-Bashforth / PLMS Methods
entities.description: Linear multi-step ODE solvers. AB1=Euler; AB2-AB4 have higher accuracy but smaller stability. PLMS = diffusion adaptation.

entities.name: Diffusion ODE Formulation
entities.description: DDIM reparameterized as ODE: d𝑥̄/dσ=𝜖̄(𝑥̄,σ), enabling numerical solvers for acceleration.

entities.name: Magnitude Score
entities.description: Sum of max-pooled latent magnitudes exceeding threshold τ. Higher = more severe divergence artifacts.

entities.name: Boundary Locus Technique
entities.description: Visualizes stability via z=s(θ)=A(e^{-iθ})/B(e^{-iθ}), θ∈[-π,π]. Plots boundary where |r|=1.

entities.name: Guided Diffusion Sampling
entities.description: Classifier or classifier-free guidance producing guided ODE: d𝑥̄/dσ=𝜖̄(𝑥̄,σ)+g(𝑥̄,σ).

entities.name: DDIM
entities.description: Denoising Diffusion Implicit Model — 1st-order non-Markovian deterministic sampler. Foundation for ODE acceleration.

entities.name: DPM-Solver++
entities.description: 2nd-order multi-step solver for guided diffusion. Target for momentum application.

entities.name: Fréchet Inception Distance (FID)
entities.description: Standard generative quality metric. Lower = better. Validates momentum improves quality, not just reduces artifacts.

entities.name: Stable Diffusion
entities.description: Latent-based text-to-image diffusion model. Primary testbed for artifact mitigation.

entities.name: ADM (Ablated Diffusion Model)
entities.description: Unconditional pixel diffusion with classifier guidance. Pixel-space evaluation baseline.

entities.name: DiT-XL
entities.description: Diffusion Transformer — latent-space diffusion with classifier-free guidance.

entities.name: Splitting Numerical Methods
entities.description: LTSP/Strang methods dividing guided ODE into subproblems. HB improves second subproblem solver.

entities.name: Polyak's Heavy Ball
entities.description: Classic optimization algorithm: x_{n+1}=x_n+α(x_n-x_{n-1})-β∇f(x_n). Inspiration for HB in diffusion sampling.

entities.name: Lie-Trotter Splitting (LTSP)
entities.description: Splitting method dividing guided ODE into two subproblems. HB momentum applied to second subproblem improves FID.

entities.name: PLMS4
entities.description: 4th-order Pseudo Linear Multi-Step solver. Primary baseline for applying momentum techniques.

entities.name: Diffusion Noise Optimization (DNO)
entities.description: Optimizes diffusion latent noise x_T via backpropagation of task criteria through full ODE solver chain. Turns pretrained motion diffusion models into universal priors without retraining.

entities.name: Latent Noise Optimization
entities.description: Rather than optimizing in raw motion space (implausible results), optimizes in diffusion latent space z=x_T where z*=argmin_z L(f(z)), decoded through ODE solver.

entities.name: Normalized Gradient Descent
entities.description: Normalizes gradients through ODE solver to unit norm. Combined with momentum produces moving average of directions robust to outliers and helps escape saddle points.

entities.name: Content Preservation via Inversion
entities.description: Inverts reference motion via DDIM inversion to get x_T_ref, uses it as optimization start, penalizes L_cont=||x_T_ref-x_T||₂ to stay close to reference during editing.

entities.name: Latent Decorrelation Loss
entities.description: Penalizes dot product between consecutive frames of x_T at multiple temporal resolutions. Prevents foot skating by discouraging correlated latent samples.

entities.name: ODE Solver Backpropagation
entities.description: Solves ODE forward to get motion, computes task loss, backpropagates through every solver step for gradient w.r.t. x_T. Memory-intensive but feasible with few DDIM steps.

entities.name: DNO vs Guided Diffusion
entities.description: Guided diffusion computes criteria on expected E[x_0|x_t] with approximation error; DNO computes on exact x after full denoising, eliminating this error.

entities.name: Relative-Root Motion Representation
entities.description: D×M matrix (D=263 features) encoding root-relative rotation/velocity, root height, joint locations/velocities/rotations, foot contact. Abstracts absolute location for generalization.

entities.name: Motion Editing via Loss Functions
entities.description: Diverse editing via composable losses: L_pose for trajectories/keyframes, L_obs for obstacle avoidance via SDF, L_cont for content preservation. Combined with balancing weights.

entities.name: MDM (Motion Diffusion Model)
entities.description: Text-to-motion diffusion model trained on HumanML3D. Primary base model for DNO.

entities.name: HumanML3D Dataset
entities.description: 44,970 motion annotations of 14,646 sequences from AMASS and HumanAct12. Standard benchmark for motion generation.

entities.name: GMD (Guided Motion Diffusion)
entities.description: Classifier-based guided diffusion for spatially-conditioned motion generation. Primary baseline compared against DNO.

entities.name: Jitter
entities.description: Mean changes in acceleration of all joints over time (m/s³). Proxy for motion smoothness.

entities.name: Foot Skating Ratio
entities.description: Proportion of frames where foot skates >2.5cm while maintaining ground contact (height<5cm). Measures trajectory-motion coherence.

entities.name: Mean Per-Joint Position Error (MPJPE)
entities.description: Average Euclidean distance between predicted and ground-truth joint positions. Standard pose reconstruction metric.

entities.name: DiffusionLight Pipeline
entities.description: In-paints chrome ball into input image via pre-trained diffusion model (SDXL+depth ControlNet), then unwraps to HDR environment map. Avoids training on limited HDR datasets.

entities.name: Iterative Inpainting
entities.description: Locates good noise maps: (1) generate N chrome balls, (2) compute pixel-wise median, (3) SDEdit on median composite, (4) repeat 2x. Leverages finding that noise maps encode semantics consistently across images.

entities.name: Noise Map Semantics
entities.description: Initial diffusion noise maps encode semantic patterns consistent across input images. Some produce disco balls, others clean chrome balls. Explains why text alone cannot encode all visual appearances.

entities.name: Exposure Bracketing LoRA
entities.description: Continuous LoRA mapping text embedding interpolations to specific EV values. Single LoRA (not per-EV) preserves scene structure via weight sharing. Text embedding: ξ_ev=ξ_o+(ev/EV_min)(ξ_d-ξ_o).

entities.name: HDR Merging via Luminance Replacement
entities.description: Replaces overexposed luminance (>0.9) with exposure-corrected values from lower EV images, retaining chroma from EV0. Avoids ghosting from standard HDR merging.

entities.name: Depth-Conditioned ControlNet
entities.description: Uses off-the-shelf depth predictor; paints white circle at center (closest to camera) for chrome ball. Feeds depth+mask+image+prompt to SDXL for reliable ball insertion.

entities.name: LoRA (Low-Rank Adaptation)
entities.description: Optimizes low-rank residual ΔW_i=A_iB_i (rank d≪m,n). Final: W_i'=W_i+αΔW_i. Used to fine-tune SDXL for chrome ball appearance and exposure control with minimal parameters.

entities.name: SDEdit
entities.description: Adds noise to image up to timestep t (B'=√α_t B+√(1-α_t)ε), then denoises back. Used in iterative inpainting for median ball refinement.

entities.name: Stable Diffusion XL (SDXL)
entities.description: Large-scale T2I diffusion model trained on billions of images. Base model for DiffusionLight pipeline with depth-conditioned ControlNet.

entities.name: Scale-Invariant RMSE (si-RMSE)
entities.description: Scale-invariant root mean square error for lighting evaluation. Normalizes out dataset-specific intensity scales.

entities.name: Angular Error
entities.description: Angular difference between predicted and ground-truth lighting. Scale-invariant metric for HDR environment map evaluation.

entities.name: StyleLight
entities.description: Auto-created during claim extraction

entities.name: DiffSDA
entities.description: Modal-agnostic diffusion framework for unsupervised sequential disentanglement. Separates static and dynamic factors using single score-matching loss. First diffusion-based method for this task.

entities.name: Dependent Static-Dynamic Modeling
entities.description: Models static and dynamic factors as interdependent (unlike prior independent models). Improves marginal expressivity by 13%, enables parallelized non-autoregressive sampling.

entities.name: Sequential Semantic Encoder
entities.description: Factorizes sequences: modality-specific backbone (U-Net/MLP) + LSTM. Last hidden→s_0 (static), all hiddens through second LSTM→d_0^{1:V} (dynamic).

entities.name: EDM-Based Decoder
entities.description: Uses EDM framework with 63 NFEs at inference. Decoder D_θ takes noisy x_t^τ and factors z_0^τ=(s_0,d_0^τ), returns denoised estimate via skip connection + AdaGN conditioning.

entities.name: Single Loss Disentanglement
entities.description: Disentanglement emerges from design, not loss terms: (1) static factor s_0 shared across time prevents capturing dynamics, (2) low-dimensional dynamic d_0^τ cannot store static features.

entities.name: Conditional Swap Task
entities.description: Standard sequential disentanglement evaluation: combine static factors of x with dynamic factors of x̂ to create new sample. Ideal swap preserves static (appearance) while transferring dynamics (motion).

entities.name: Zero-Shot Disentanglement
entities.description: First demonstration: model trained on one dataset (VoxCeleb) performs conditional swap on unseen dataset (MUG/CelebV-HQ).

entities.name: PCA Multifactor Disentanglement
entities.description: PCA on sampled static vectors reveals principal components controlling interpretable attributes (gender, skin tone, blurriness). Traversal: s̄=((s-μ)/σ+α·v_i·√h)·σ+μ.

entities.name: Average Euclidean Distance (AED)
entities.description: Unsupervised metric for object/static preservation in swaps. Computes distances between latent representations of images.

entities.name: Average Keypoint Distance (AKD)
entities.description: Unsupervised metric for motion/dynamic preservation in swaps. Computes distances between selected keypoints in images.

entities.name: Equal Error Rate (EER)
entities.description: Standard speech metric. Static EER measures speaker identity capture; Dynamic EER measures content capture without identity. Goal: low Static EER, high Dynamic EER, maximize gap.

entities.name: SPYL
entities.description: Prior SOTA sequential disentanglement method using VAEs. Primary baseline for DiffSDA comparison.

entities.name: DBSE
entities.description: Sequential disentanglement by extracting static info from single sequence element. State-of-the-art baseline.

entities.name: Neon (Negative Extrapolation)
entities.description: Post-hoc model improvement method: fine-tune on self-generated data, then negatively extrapolate away from degraded weights.

entities.name: Model Autophagy Disorder (MAD)
entities.description: Model collapse where iterative self-training on synthetic data causes rapid degradation in sample quality and diversity.

entities.name: Negative Extrapolation
entities.description: Parameter merge θ_Neon = θ_r + w(θ_r - θ_s) that reverses the synthetic gradient direction.

entities.name: Gradient Anti-Alignment
entities.description: The insight that synthetic gradients are anti-aligned with population gradients, enabling Neon to work.

entities.name: Self-Training (Generative)
entities.description: Fine-tuning a generative model on its own synthesized outputs. Causes MAD/model collapse; repurposed by Neon.

entities.name: Precision-Recall Trade-off (Neon)
entities.description: Neon's mechanism: redistributes probability mass from over-represented to under-represented modes.

entities.name: xAR-L
entities.description: State-of-the-art autoregressive image generation model. Neon elevates xAR-L from FID 1.28 to 1.02 on ImageNet-256.

entities.name: Inductive Moment Matching (IMM)
entities.description: Few-step generative framework learning direct transitions via moment matching. Neon works on IMM models.

entities.name: Fréchet Inception Distance (FID)
entities.description: Primary evaluation metric for image generation quality.

entities.name: Mode-Seeking Sampler
entities.description: Inference samplers that emphasize high-density regions via monotone reweighting of the reference model.

entities.name: Data-Constrained Pre-training
entities.sub_type: pre-training with fixed data and unlimited compute
entities.description: Pre-training regime where data is fixed and finite but compute is unlimited, inverse of Chinchilla regime.

entities.name: Loss Asymptote Evaluation
entities.description: Evaluating recipes by the limit of their power law as N approaches infinity rather than at fixed compute budgets.

entities.name: Regularized Parameter Scaling
entities.description: Jointly tuning weight decay (up to 30x standard), learning rate, and epoch count for monotone power-law scaling.

entities.name: Ensembling Recipe
entities.description: Averaging logits of K independently trained models. Achieves lower loss asymptote than parameter scaling.

entities.name: Joint Scaling Recipe
entities.description: Composing parameter scaling and ensemble scaling by taking both N and K to infinity.

entities.name: Overfitting in Data-Constrained Regime
entities.description: With fixed data, too many epochs or parameters cause loss to increase - model memorizes rather than generalizes.

entities.name: Monotone Scaling Law
entities.description: Regularized data-constrained pre-training follows L = A/N^α + E with exponent ~1.0, steeper than Chinchilla's 0.34.

entities.name: Ensemble Distillation
entities.description: Distilling an ensemble of K models into a single student, retaining 83% of ensembling benefit with 8x smaller inference cost.

entities.name: Data Scaling Laws
entities.description: Extrapolation of loss asymptotes across token budgets, predicting data efficiency gains persist at larger token counts.

entities.name: GLASS Flows
entities.description: Gaussian Latent Sufficient Statistic Flows: ODE-based method for sampling Markov transitions using inner flow matching from pre-trained weights without retraining.

entities.name: Transition Kernel
entities.description: p_{t'|t}(x_{t'}|x_t): Markov transition probability between two timesteps in a diffusion/flow process.

entities.name: Reward Alignment
entities.description: Inference-time optimization adding reward objectives to pre-trained flow/diffusion models.

entities.name: ODE vs SDE Sampling Trade-off
entities.description: ODEs are efficient but deterministic; SDEs are stochastic but slow. GLASS eliminates this trade-off.

entities.name: Inner Flow Matching
entities.description: A flow matching model nested inside another to sample transitions, derived from pre-trained outer model using sufficient statistic transformation.

entities.name: Sufficient Statistic
entities.description: The conditional expectation of clean data given a noisy sample is a sufficient statistic for the transition distribution.

entities.name: Feynman-Kac Steering
entities.description: Reward alignment algorithm using sequential Monte Carlo with transition kernels. GLASS accelerates it.

entities.name: Knowledge Graph
entities.description: A graph of data intended to accumulate and convey knowledge of the real world

entities.name: Directed Edge-labelled Graph
entities.description: A graph data model with nodes and directed labelled edges

entities.name: Heterogeneous Graph
entities.description: A graph where each node and edge is assigned one type

entities.name: Property Graph
entities.description: A graph model allowing property-value pairs and labels on nodes and edges

entities.name: Resource Description Framework (RDF)
entities.description: Standard data model for directed edge-labelled graphs

entities.description: Web Ontology Language for representing ontologies

entities.name: Knowledge Graph Embedding
entities.description: Machine learning approach to learn low-dimensional representations

entities.name: TransE
entities.description: Seminal translational embedding model

entities.name: Graph Neural Network
entities.description: Neural network architectures for graph-structured data

entities.name: Rule Mining
entities.description: Discovering meaningful patterns as rules from graphs

entities.name: Description Logics
entities.description: Family of logics for formalizing knowledge graph semantics

entities.description: Google's knowledge graph announced in 2012

entities.name: Open Knowledge Graph
entities.description: Knowledge graph published online for public good

entities.name: Enterprise Knowledge Graph
entities.description: Knowledge graph internal to a company

entities.description: Application of analytical algorithms to analyze graph topology

entities.description: Early neural model for knowledge graph embeddings

entities.name: Closed World Assumption
entities.description: Assumption that what is not known is assumed false

entities.name: Open World Assumption
entities.description: Assumption that relations may exist without being described

entities.name: Shapes Graph
entities.description: Set of interrelated shapes defining constraints on data graph nodes

entities.name: Reification
entities.description: Representation technique for describing edges in a graph

entities.description: Auto-created during claim extraction

entities.name: Future Research Directions
entities.description: Auto-created during claim extraction

entities.name: Speculative Decoding
entities.description: Draft-then-verify paradigm: a small draft model proposes tokens, a large target model verifies and selectively accepts them.

entities.name: Joint Intractability
entities.description: Computing full joint probabilities over all possible decoding paths is computationally infeasible in sequence-level verification.

entities.name: Hierarchical Speculative Decoding (HSD)
entities.description: Provably lossless verification method: organizes multiple resampling distributions hierarchically across levels, maximizing accepted tokens.

entities.name: Generalized Divergence
entities.description: D_Ω'(p,q) = sum over Ω' of max{p(ω)−q(ω), 0}: measures total deficient mass within a subset.

entities.name: Branch Divergence
entities.description: Divergence computed over a branch of all possible next-token continuations of a prefix.

entities.name: Partial Distribution Recovery
entities.description: Theorem: a target distribution over subset Ω' can be fully recovered via resampling iff D_Ω'(p,q) ≤ D_Ω'(q,p).

entities.name: Hierarchical Branch Resampling
entities.description: HSD's core mechanism: scan backward, find point where excess mass ≥ deficient mass, accept tokens, resample once from hierarchy.

entities.name: Capped Branch Resampling
entities.description: Efficiency optimization using prefix-level cap ratios to avoid computing full branch divergences at every position.

entities.name: Token-wise Verification
entities.description: Standard SD verification: each token independently accepted with probability min(1, p/q) or rejected and resampled.

entities.name: Blockwise Verification
entities.description: Prior lossless joint verification method that verifies blocks of tokens jointly but falls short of ideal acceptance rates.

entities.name: EAGLE-3
entities.description: State-of-the-art multi-draft speculative decoding framework that predicts drafts from target model hidden features.

entities.name: Multi-Draft Speculative Decoding
entities.description: Generating multiple candidate draft tokens at each position via tree attention, increasing acceptance probability.

entities.name: Lossless Verification
entities.description: Verification methods guaranteeing exact recovery of the target model's output distribution.

entities.name: GraphRAG
entities.description: Graph-based RAG approach combining knowledge graph generation and query-focused summarization

entities.name: Vector RAG
entities.description: Conventional RAG using text embeddings for semantic similarity retrieval

entities.description: Summarization task focused on answering specific questions over entire corpora

entities.name: Leiden Community Detection

entities.description: Evaluation technique using LLMs to judge relative answer quality

entities.name: Adaptive Benchmarking

entities.description: Two complementary structures: vertical knowledge graph and horizontal workflow graph

entities.description: Semantic network of entities, relationships, constraints, and temporal metadata

entities.name: Horizontal Workflow Graph
entities.description: DAG of reasoning and execution nodes that structures decisions into steps

entities.name: Agent Harness

entities.name: Latent Speech-Text Transformer (LST)
entities.description: Architecture aggregating speech tokens into latent patches as higher-level autoregressive units.

entities.name: Latent Speech Patching
entities.description: Core mechanism dynamically grouping sequences of discrete speech tokens into higher-level patch representations.

entities.name: Modality Imbalance (Speech-Text)
entities.description: Speech tokens require up to 3 orders of magnitude more tokens than text for equivalent semantic content.

entities.name: Generative Spoken Language Modeling (GSLM)
entities.description: Task of training autoregressive LMs on discretized speech tokens for raw speech understanding and generation.

entities.name: Byte-Latent Transformer (BLT)
entities.description: Architecture that dynamically groups bytes into patches for efficient text processing.

entities.name: Speech-Text Representational Alignment
entities.description: Goal of making speech and text modalities interchangeable in a model without performance difference.

entities.name: Curriculum Patching
entities.description: Training strategy eliminating need for text-speech alignment timestamps during inference.

entities.name: Speech HellaSwag
entities.description: Speech version of HellaSwag benchmark using Kokoro TTS for commonsense reasoning evaluation.

entities.name: Static Patching
entities.description: Fixed-length non-overlapping speech patch segmentation.

entities.name: SpiritLM
entities.description: Interleaved spoken and written language model from Nguyen et al. 2025.

entities.name: BPE SpeechLLM
entities.description: SpeechLLM baseline using BPE tokenization on speech tokens.

entities.name: Mixture-of-Experts (MoE)
entities.description: Sparse activation architecture where only a subset of expert parameters are activated per token.

entities.name: Equal Resource Comparison
entities.description: Comparing MoE vs dense under strictly identical constraints: same total parameter count, training compute, data budget.

entities.name: Optimal Activation Rate (AR)
entities.description: The proportion of parameters activated per token in MoE. Found to be approximately 20 percent.

entities.name: Data Reuse Strategy
entities.description: MoE models benefit from more data than dense under equal compute; reuse resolves the trade-off.

entities.name: Dense vs Sparse Architecture
entities.description: Dense models activate all parameters per token; sparse MoE activate only a subset.

entities.name: Sparse Activation
entities.description: Only a fraction of model parameters are used for each input token, controlled by the activation rate.

entities.name: Dense LLM Baseline
entities.description: Standard dense Transformer language model used as comparison baseline.

entities.name: Matching Models
entities.description: Umbrella term for generative models that learn via conditional matching objectives.

entities.name: Universal Matching (UM) Loss
entities.description: A unified loss function encompassing all matching model training objectives.

entities.name: Score Identity Distillation (SID)
entities.description: Prior data-free distillation method for diffusion models using squared L2 distance.

entities.name: Flow Generator Matching (FGM)
entities.description: Prior data-free distillation for flow matching models minimizing squared L2 distance between drifts.

entities.name: Universal Inverse Distillation (UID)
entities.description: Unified min-max distillation framework encompassing SID FGM and IBMD using linearization technique.

entities.name: RealUID
entities.description: Extension of UID that incorporates real data into distillation without GANs via RealUM loss.

entities.name: Linearization Technique
entities.description: Reformulates intractable squared norm of expectation as max over auxiliary function.

entities.name: Fake Function
entities.description: Auxiliary function trained alongside generator in UID RealUID min-max game.

entities.name: RealUM Loss (alpha beta)
entities.description: Real Unified Matching loss parametrized by alpha beta controlling real vs generated data balance.

entities.name: Inverse Optimization Connection
entities.description: UID loss structure matches inverse optimization.

entities.name: GAN-Free Real Data Incorporation
entities.description: RealUID incorporates real data by modifying matching loss only, no adversarial training.

entities.name: One-Step Generator
entities.description: Distilled student model mapping latent noise directly to data samples in one forward pass.

entities.name: Graph Neural Diffusion (GRAND)
entities.description: New class of GNNs approaching deep learning on graphs as a continuous diffusion process.

entities.name: Diffusion Equation on Graphs
entities.description: Continuous PDE adapted to graphs using discrete gradient and divergence operators.

entities.name: Explicit vs Implicit Schemes (GRAND)
entities.description: Forward Euler and backward Euler discretizations of graph diffusion PDE.

entities.name: Multi-step and Adaptive Schemes (GRAND)
entities.description: Runge-Kutta, Adams-Bashford, Adams-Moulton methods for graph diffusion ODE.

entities.name: Stability of Graph Diffusion
entities.description: Stability conditions for explicit tau less than 1 and implicit unconditionally stable.

entities.name: Oversmoothing and Bottlenecks
entities.description: Common plagues of GNNs addressed by GRAND through discretisation and rewiring.

entities.name: Graph Rewiring (GRAND)
entities.description: Modifying edge set used for message passing independently of input graph.

entities.name: Parameter Sharing Across Layers (GRAND)
entities.description: GRAND shares parameters across all layers, making it lightweight and data-efficient.

entities.name: Message Passing Paradigm (GRAND)
entities.description: Message passing modelled as a diffusion differential equation. Standard GNNs are single-step explicit Euler.

entities.name: Auto-Compressing Networks (ACNs)
entities.description: Architecture with long skip connections from each layer to output, enabling auto-compression

entities.name: Residual Networks (ResNets)
entities.description: Architecture using short residual skip connections

entities.name: Feedforward Networks (FFNs)
entities.description: Traditional sequential feedforward neural networks

entities.name: Auto-Compression
entities.description: Property where information is organically compressed into early layers during training

entities.name: Direct Gradient (DG)
entities.description: Gradient component that directly connects from the output to each layer

entities.name: Network-mediated Gradient (NG)
entities.description: Gradient component scaled by network weights that backpropagates through the network

entities.name: Layer-wise Training
entities.description: Training pattern where layers are trained sequentially from bottom to top

entities.name: Continual Learning
entities.description: Training models on a sequence of tasks without access to past data

entities.name: Transfer Learning
entities.description: Applying knowledge learned from one task to a related task

entities.name: AC-BERT
entities.description: Auto-compressing variant of BERT

entities.name: Conditional Flow Matching (CFM)
entities.description: Standard training objective for flow matching that uses stochastic conditional velocity targets

entities.name: Empirical Flow Matching (EFM)
entities.description: Training objective that regresses against the closed-form optimal velocity field estimated via Monte Carlo

entities.name: Flow Matching
entities.description: Generative modeling framework that constructs a probability path between a source and target distribution via a learned velocity field

entities.name: Optimal Velocity Field (u^*)
entities.description: The minimizer of the flow matching loss, available in closed-form when using empirical data distribution

entities.name: Conditional Velocity Field (u^cond)
entities.description: The conditional velocity field used as a regression target in conditional flow matching

entities.name: Generalization-Through-Variance Hypothesis
entities.description: The hypothesis that generalization in flow matching arises from the stochastic/noisy nature of the conditional flow matching loss

entities.name: Imagenette
entities.description: Subset of ImageNet used for dimensionality analysis experiments

entities.name: Two-Moons Dataset
entities.description: Low-dimensional (d=2) toy dataset used to demonstrate dimensionality effects on flow matching

entities.name: Flow Matching Memorization
entities.description: The phenomenon where flow matching models exactly reproduce training samples rather than generating novel samples

entities.name: EFM Monte Carlo Estimator (hat{u}_M^*)
entities.description: Monte Carlo estimator of the optimal velocity field with lower variance than standard CFM target

entities.name: Flow Matching Generalization
entities.description: The ability of flow matching models to generate novel samples not present in the training data

entities.name: Hybrid Sampling Model
entities.description: Piecewise trajectory following the optimal velocity for t in [0,tau] and learned velocity for t in [tau,1]

entities.name: Optimal Transport Flow Matching (OT-CFM)
entities.description: Flow matching variant using minibatch optimal transport couplings

entities.name: On the Closed-Form of Flow Matching: Generalization Does Not Arise from Target Stochasticity
entities.description: Auto-created during claim extraction

entities.name: MeanFlow
