entities.name: MMDiT blocks
entities.description: Multimodal Diffusion Transformer blocks integrating text and video streams

entities.name: SSIM
entities.description: Structural Similarity Index for single-frame evaluation quality

entities.name: DiffFit
entities.description: Two-stage latent diffusion framework decoupling geometric alignment and texture refinement for VTON

entities.name: CAW-UNet
entities.description: Cross Attention Warping UNet for geometry-aware garment warping using multi-source feature extraction and cross-attention fusion

entities.name: Cross-Modal Conditional Diffusion UNet
entities.description: Second-stage UNet that integrates warped garment, original garment, and person image for photorealistic rendering

entities.name: Pose-Adaptive Diffusion Network
entities.description: First-stage network that learns semantic correspondences between garments and body shapes in latent space

entities.name: VITON-HD
entities.description: High-resolution VTON dataset with 13,679 frontal-view female and upper-body garment pairs

entities.name: DressCode
entities.description: Multi-category VTON dataset with 53,795 pairs covering upper-body, lower-body, and dresses

entities.name: IGPair
entities.description: Large-scale VTON dataset with 324,857 high-quality image pairs at ultra-high resolution

entities.name: Image-based Virtual Try-On
entities.description: Task of transferring a clothing item onto a target person image for realistic synthesis

entities.name: SSIM
entities.description: Structural Similarity Index for measuring reconstruction quality

entities.name: FID
entities.description: Frechet Inception Distance for assessing realism and distributional consistency

entities.name: Q-Former
entities.description: Trainable projection module that maps image features to unified embedding tokens for cross-attention injection

entities.name: LPIPS
entities.description: Learned Perceptual Image Patch Similarity for perceptual quality evaluation

entities.name: OmniVTON
entities.description: First training-free universal VTON framework decoupling garment and pose conditioning for both in-shop and in-the-wild scenarios

entities.name: Structured Garment Morphing
entities.description: Training-free garment deformation using pseudo-person generation, multi-part semantic correspondence, and localized transformations

entities.name: Spectral Pose Injection
entities.description: Frequency-aware pose injection using FFT-based decomposition to preserve low-frequency pose structure while removing high-frequency texture contamination

entities.name: Continuous Boundary Stitching
entities.description: Bidirectional semantic context manipulation between garment and garment-infused images via attention modulation for seamless boundaries

entities.name: Pseudo-Person Image Generation
entities.description: Attention modulation method that generates human image from garment by integrating person semantics via key-value injection

entities.name: VITON-HD
entities.description: In-shop VTON dataset with 2,032 upper garment-model test pairs

entities.name: DressCode
entities.description: Multi-category VTON dataset with 5,400 test samples across upper, lower, and dresses

entities.name: DeepFashion2
entities.description: In-the-wild dataset used for cross-scenario evaluation with 2,089 test images

entities.name: Image-based Virtual Try-On
entities.description: Task of transferring garment images onto target human bodies to generate natural-looking results

entities.name: DDIM Inversion
entities.description: Deterministic inversion that preserves structural information by replacing random noise with source image inversion noise

entities.name: Multi-Human VTON
entities.description: Extension of VTON to multiple individuals in a single scene enabling group try-on

entities.name: FID
entities.description: Frechet Inception Distance for measuring similarity between generated and real image distributions

entities.name: DreamVVT
entities.description: Two-stage stage-wise DiT-based framework for video virtual try-on using keyframe try-on and VLM-guided video generation

entities.name: Multi-frame Try-on Model
entities.description: DiT-based model with LoRA adapters that generates consistent multi-frame try-on results from garment images and VLM descriptions

entities.name: Keyframe Sampling
entities.description: Algorithm selecting frames with significant motion changes using cosine distance of skeletal joint direction vectors

entities.name: Multi-modal Guided Video Generation
entities.description: Second-stage model using pretrained image-to-video DiT with LoRA adapters conditioned on keyframes, pose, and text

entities.name: LoRA Adapters
entities.description: Low-rank adaptation layers comprising only 10% of trainable parameters for efficient fine-tuning of pretrained models

entities.name: VLM (Vision-Language Model)
entities.description: Vision-language model used for generating textual descriptions and text alignment for keyframe try-on

entities.name: Wild-TryOnBench
entities.description: In-the-wild benchmark with 81 samples encompassing subject/camera movement, scene changes, and diverse garments

entities.name: ViViD-S dataset
entities.description: Indoor scenario evaluation dataset with 180 samples for video virtual try-on

entities.name: Video Virtual Try-On
entities.description: Task of rendering arbitrary garments onto characters within video sequences in unconstrained scenarios

entities.name: VFID
entities.description: Video Frechet Inception Distance for evaluating video quality with I3D and ResNeXt feature extractors

entities.name: RTMPose
entities.description: Robust and efficient pose representation model used for both real and cartoon character pose estimation

entities.name: Video LLM
entities.description: Video large language model used to extract fine-grained action descriptions and high-level visual information

entities.name: OMFA
entities.description: One Model For All: unified diffusion framework for both virtual try-on and try-off, mask-free, using Bidirectional Tweedie Diffusion and SMPL-X pose conditioning.

entities.name: Bidirectional Tweedie Diffusion
entities.description: A target-selective denoising process in continuous latent space that unifies try-on and try-off by diffusing only the selected target latent while retaining remaining latents as observed conditions.

entities.name: SMPL-X
entities.description: Low-dimensional parametric human body model using shape parameters beta and pose parameters theta to produce a 3D human mesh, used for structural conditioning in OMFA.

entities.name: VITON-HD
entities.description: Dataset containing 13,679 image pairs of frontal half-body models and corresponding upper-body garments, with 11,647 for training and 2,032 for testing.

entities.name: DeepFashion-MultiModal
entities.description: Dataset where each sample includes images of person, garment, and a pair of target images in two poses; approximately 40,000 training samples and 1,100 test samples.

entities.name: Virtual Try-On (VTON)
entities.description: Task of generating photorealistic images of a person wearing a desired garment.

entities.name: Virtual Try-Off (VTOFF)
entities.description: Task of recovering a canonical garment image from a person wearing the clothing.

entities.name: Mask-Free Framework
entities.description: No segmentation or parsing masks required during inference; OMFA operates in a practical mask-free setting requiring only a single portrait image and a target garment image.

entities.name: SSIM
entities.description: Structural Similarity Index Measure used for evaluating paired setting similarity between synthesized and ground-truth images.

entities.name: FID
entities.description: Frechet Inception Distance used for evaluating visual quality in both paired and unpaired settings.

entities.name: LLM-based Image Scoring
entities.description: Comprehensive score from GPT-4o-mini for try-on result quality, ranging from 0 to 10.

entities.name: Exhibition Garments
entities.description: Clean garment templates that existing methods rely on; OMFA eliminates this requirement.

entities.name: Voost
entities.description: Unified and scalable framework that jointly learns virtual try-on and try-off with a single diffusion transformer, using token-level concatenation and task token conditioning.

entities.name: Attention Temperature Scaling
entities.description: Dynamic temperature scaling scheme that adapts attention sharpness to both absolute token length changes and relative spatial imbalance between masked and conditioning regions.

entities.name: Self-Corrective Sampling
entities.description: Leverages bidirectional consistency between try-on and try-off tasks within the unified model to iteratively refine predictions.

entities.name: DressCode
entities.description: High-resolution multi-category virtual try-on dataset containing image pairs of in-shop garments and corresponding person images.

entities.name: VITON-HD
entities.description: Standard benchmark dataset for virtual try-on containing high-resolution image pairs of frontal half-body models and upper-body garments.

entities.name: Diffusion Transformer (DiT)
entities.description: Scalable transformer architecture for diffusion-based generation used as the backbone of Voost.

entities.name: Task Token
entities.description: Concatenation of a mode token (on/off) specifying generation direction and a category token (upper/lower/full) encoding garment type, passed to the transformer as additional conditioning.

entities.name: Flow Matching
entities.description: Training formulation where the model learns a time-dependent velocity field that transports samples from data distribution to noise along a continuous path.

entities.name: Attention-Only Tuning
entities.description: Fine-tuning only attention modules while freezing other parameters, achieving best overall performance compared to full fine-tuning, single-block training, and LoRA.

entities.name: FID
entities.description: Frechet Inception Distance used for evaluating visual fidelity and realism.

entities.name: SSIM
entities.description: Structural Similarity Index Measure used for evaluating structural consistency.

entities.name: OmniTry
entities.description: Unified mask-free framework extending VTON beyond garments to any wearable objects including jewelries and accessories, using a two-staged training pipeline.

entities.name: Traceless Erasing
entities.description: Strategy to eliminate artifacts from erasure by applying image-to-image translation to subtly re-paint the entire image after erasure, preventing the model from learning shortcuts.

entities.name: Two-Stage Training Pipeline
entities.description: Stage 1 leverages large-scale unpaired images for mask-free localization; Stage 2 fine-tunes with paired images for ID consistency.

entities.name: Location Adapter
entities.description: LoRA-based adapter for stage-1 training that enables mask-free localization by adapting the inpainting model.

entities.name: Identity Adapter
entities.description: LoRA-based adapter for stage-2 training that controls the consistency of object appearance.

entities.name: OmniTry-Bench
entities.description: Comprehensive benchmark covering 12 common types of wearable objects across 4 major classes: clothes, shoes, jewelries, and accessories.

entities.name: Masked Full-Attention
entities.description: Attention mechanism where object image tokens are appended to the DiT sequence with blocked information flow from generated try-on to object image.

entities.name: Two-Stream Adapters
entities.description: Separate adapters for person image tokens (Location Adapter) and object image tokens (Identity Adapter), switched by token source identification.

entities.name: M-DINO
entities.description: Masked DINO similarity for evaluating object consistency between cropped objects from try-on and object images.

entities.name: M-CLIP-I
entities.description: Masked CLIP image similarity for evaluating semantic alignment between try-on and object images.

entities.name: G-Accuracy
entities.description: Success rate of visual grounding model detecting the object in the generated try-on image.

entities.name: Diffusion Transformer (DiT)
entities.description: Scalable transformer architecture used as the backbone for OmniTry's diffusion-based generation.

entities.name: JCo-MVTON
entities.description: Jointly Controllable Multi-Modal Diffusion Transformer for Mask-Free Virtual Try-On, using MM-DiT backbone with multi-modal condition fusion.

entities.name: Multi-Modal Diffusion Transformer (MM-DiT)
entities.description: Diffusion transformer backbone that integrates diverse control signals including reference image and garment image directly into the denoising process through dedicated conditional pathways.

entities.name: Bi-directional Generation Strategy
entities.description: Two complementary pathways for constructing training data: mask-based model generates reference images, Try-Off model synthesizes garment data, with manual screening.

entities.name: Masked-Attention Mechanism
entities.description: Specialized attention masking to mitigate inter-branch interference between conditional pathways in MM-DiT.

entities.name: DressCode
entities.description: Public benchmark dataset used for evaluating JCo-MVTON's state-of-the-art performance.

entities.name: Try-Off Model
entities.description: Model sharing identical architecture with JCo-MVTON, trained via self-supervision to synthesize garment data for the bi-directional generation strategy.

entities.name: ReferenceNet
entities.description: Previous state-of-the-art conditioning approach that JCo-MVTON surpasses using MM-DiT.

entities.name: Cyclic Data Pipeline
entities.description: Self-sustaining pipeline that iteratively improves both model performance and data quality through successive iterations of model refinement and data augmentation.

entities.name: OOTDiffusion
entities.description: Diffusion-based VTON baseline that formulates garment transfer as a conditional denoising process.

entities.name: CatVTON
entities.description: Category-aware transfer mechanism VTON baseline that classifies garments into semantic categories.

entities.name: Kling
entities.description: Commercial virtual try-on system compared against JCo-MVTON in human evaluation.

entities.name: Dress&Dance
entities.description: Video diffusion framework that generates 5-second 24 FPS virtual try-on videos at 1152x720 resolution, guided by a reference motion video.

entities.name: CondNet
entities.description: Novel conditioning network that leverages cross-attention to unify multi-modal inputs for enhanced garment registration and motion fidelity.

entities.name: Video Refiner
entities.description: Auto-regressive video refiner stage that upsamples the video from 8 FPS to 24 FPS while refining appearance details.

entities.name: Garment Warm-up Training
entities.description: Curriculum learning based training phase that guides the model to quickly learn garment registration before full training.

entities.name: Multi-Stage Progressive Training
entities.description: Stage-wise multi-phase resolution progressive training strategy that gradually increases resolution and condition complexity.

entities.name: Synthetic Triplets
entities.description: Synthetically generated unpaired triplets that eliminate the need for intermediates like agnostic masks or Dense Poses.

entities.name: Kling Video 1.6
entities.description: State-of-the-art commercial video generation model used as a baseline comparison.

entities.name: CogVideoX
entities.description: Open-source image-to-video generation model used in baseline pipeline for animation from try-on images.

entities.name: Fashion-VDM
entities.description: Video diffusion model for virtual try-on used as a video try-on baseline comparison.

entities.name: PSNR
entities.description: Peak Signal-to-Noise Ratio used for quantitative evaluation of generated video quality against ground truth.

entities.name: GPTTry-On Score
entities.description: GPT-based evaluation metric for garment try-on fidelity and quality.

entities.name: CaP-VTON
entities.description: Clothing Agnostic Pre-Inpainting Virtual Try-On: integrates DressCode-based multi-category masking and Stable Diffusion-based skin inpainting to address Leffa's limitations.

entities.name: Leffa
entities.description: Learning Flow Fields in Attention method applying flow field regularization to attention in diffusion models for detailed texture preservation.

entities.name: Generate Skin Module
entities.description: Stable Diffusion-based skin inpainting pipeline that removes existing clothing silhouettes and naturally restores exposed skin areas.

entities.name: DressCode Multi-Category Masking
entities.description: Multi-category masking pipeline based on DressCode dataset covering full-body clothing categories integrated into Leffa's pipeline.

entities.name: Modified Leffa Pipeline
entities.description: Ensemble approach combining DressCode model for inpainting masking with VITON-HD model for high-quality diffusion synthesis.

entities.name: MajicMix Realistic Model
entities.description: Stable Diffusion 1.5-based high-quality checkpoint model optimized for Asian skin tones, used for skin inpainting.

entities.name: SCHP (Self-Correction for Human Parsing)
entities.description: Model used to subdivide the semantic parts of the human body for accurate masking in CaP-VTON pipeline.

entities.name: Normal Output Rate
entities.description: Statistical-based indicator measuring percentage of cases where normal short-sleeved silhouette is accurately synthesized.

entities.name: VITON-HD
entities.description: Dataset used for training and evaluation, specializing in upper-body clothing images with FID 4.54 in CaP-VTON evaluation.

entities.name: DressCode
entities.description: Multi-category dataset including tops, bottoms, and dresses, used for multi-category masking in CaP-VTON.

entities.name: ART-VITON
entities.description: Measurement-guided diffusion framework that reformulates VITON as a linear inverse problem, integrating prior-based initialization and artifact-free measurement-guided sampling.

entities.name: Prior-based Initialization
entities.description: Residual prior-based initialization that mitigates train-test mismatch by starting from a single DDPM denoising step instead of pure Gaussian noise.

entities.name: Artifact-Free Measurement-Guided Sampling
entities.description: Combines data consistency, frequency-level correction, and periodic standard denoising to enforce measurement constraints while maintaining artifact-free synthesis.

entities.name: Data Consistency
entities.description: Preserves semantic coherence by interpolating latents toward reference-informed estimates, reducing semantic drift across regions.

entities.name: Frequency-Level Correction
entities.description: Restores high-frequency details lost during VAE compression by injecting high-frequency components via per-channel Fourier transform.

entities.name: Periodic Standard Denoising
entities.description: Standard denoising steps applied periodically to realign trajectories with the LDM data manifold and prevent over-constrained solutions.

entities.name: VITON-HD
entities.description: Dataset with 11,647 training and 2,032 test pairs of frontal-view female upper-body images at 1024x768 resolution.

entities.name: SHHQ-1.0
entities.description: 40K high-quality full-body images dataset used for cross-domain generalization evaluation.

entities.name: DressCode
entities.description: Full-body images dataset with upper/lower/dress items used for evaluation.

entities.name: Boundary Artifacts
entities.description: Spatial discontinuities at region interfaces from post-hoc replacement, manifesting as color mismatches and broken textures.

entities.name: StableVITON
entities.description: LDM-based VITON model that strengthens garment-human interaction via zero cross-attention block in ControlNet.

entities.name: TReg
entities.description: Hybrid stochastic inverse solver that alternates between pixel- and latent-space refinements.

entities.name: DreamSampler
entities.description: Hybrid stochastic inverse solver integrating pixel-space and latent-space optimization for diffusion sampling.

entities.name: CatVTON
entities.description: Simple efficient VTON. Spatially concatenates garment+person as UNet input. No text encoder/cross-attn/ReferenceNet. 899M total, 49.57M trainable (5.5%). ICLR 2025.

entities.name: Spatial Concatenation for VTON
entities.description: Concatenating garment+person images along spatial dim as denoising UNet input, keeping them in shared latent space without extra encoding modules or warping.

entities.name: Mask-Free Try-On
entities.description: VTON requiring only person image + garment reference. No mask, pose, or parsing. Trained on synthetic pairs from mask-based model.

entities.name: Mask-Based Try-On
entities.description: VTON using binary cloth-agnostic mask, removing garment from person then inpainting target garment.

entities.name: Denoising Diffusion Probabilistic Models (DDPM)
entities.description: Generative framework reversing gradual noising. z_T∼N(0,1) denoised T steps: z_{t-1}=UNet(z_t,cond). Standard formulation used by CatVTON as the base generative process.

entities.name: Denoising UNet Architecture
entities.description: Core LDM network. Alternating ResNet blocks (local conv features) + transformer blocks (global self-attention). CatVTON simplifies by removing cross-attention text conditioning.

entities.name: Self-Attention for Diffusion Fine-tuning
entities.description: Self-attention layers in UNet transformer blocks. CatVTON identifies these as the most critical for VTON adaptation, training only 49.57M out of 899.06M (5.5%) with comparable results to full fine-tuning.

entities.name: Cross-Attention Text Conditioning
entities.description: Standard LDM mechanism injecting CLIP text embeddings into UNet via cross-attn. CatVTON removes entirely: text is redundant for image-based VTON, saves 167M params.

entities.name: Classifier-Free Guidance
entities.description: ε_CFG = ε_uncond + s·(ε_cond − ε_uncond). CatVTON uses 10% conditional dropout, s=2.5 optimal at inference. s>3.5 causes color distortion and high-frequency noise (Ho & Salimans, 2022).

entities.name: DREAM Training
entities.description: Training strategy with λ balancing distortion vs perceptual quality. λ→∞ disables; small λ→smooth; large λ→high-freq artifacts. CatVTON optimal λ=10 (Zhou et al., 2024).

entities.name: T-Step Iterative Denoising
entities.description: z_T∼N(0,1) progressively denoised T timesteps: z_{t-1}=UNet(z_t ⊙ conditions). Output z_0 decoded by VAE to pixels. CatVTON input: concatenated noise+latent person+latent garment.

entities.name: Stable Diffusion v1.5
entities.description: Pre-trained latent diffusion model (Rombach et al., 2021). CatVTON uses inpainting version (mask-based) and InstructPix2Pix version (mask-free) as initialization, leveraging robust priors.

entities.name: VAE for Latent Diffusion
entities.description: Encoder: image→z∈R^{4×H/8×W/8} (8× down). Decoder: z→image. Standard LDM component. CatVTON encodes garment+person via VAE before spatial concat in latent space.

entities.name: MSE Diffusion Loss
entities.description: L = ||ε − ε_θ(z_t, conditions)||². Standard DDPM objective predicting added noise ε. CatVTON uses MSE for both mask-based and mask-free training.

entities.name: ReferenceNet / Dual-UNet
entities.description: Replicates UNet weights for separate garment feature extraction at multiple scales. Used by OOTDiffusion, IDM-VTON, OutfitAnyone. CatVTON removes this entirely.

entities.name: IP-Adapter
entities.description: CLIP image encoder→subject features→cross-attention into diffusion (Ye et al., 2023). CatVTON argues pre-trained encoders lose fine garment details needed for VTON.

entities.name: ControlNet
entities.description: Spatial conditioning (pose, depth, edge) for diffusion without weight modification (Zhang et al., 2023). CatVTON removes need for external condition inputs.

entities.name: LoRA
entities.description: Parameter-efficient fine-tuning via low-rank matrices (Hu et al., 2021). Alternative to CatVTON's selective self-attention training.

entities.name: DreamBooth
entities.description: Fine-tunes diffusion on few subject images (Ruiz et al., 2022). Referenced as subject-driven generation approach.

entities.name: AdamW
entities.description: Adam with decoupled weight decay (Loshchilov & Hutter, 2019). CatVTON: batch 128, lr=1e-5, 16K steps @ 512×384 on 8×A800 (~10h).

entities.name: AnyDoor
entities.description: DINOv2+ControlNet for subject feature extraction (Chen et al., 2023). High-quality object-level customization referenced in related work.

entities.name: DINOv2
entities.description: Self-supervised ViT (Oquab et al., 2023). Used by other VTON methods as garment feature extractor. CatVTON argues pre-trained encoders are not optimized for detail preservation.

entities.name: CLIP Vision Encoder
entities.description: CLIP visual encoder (Radford et al., 2021). Used by IP-Adapter/VTON methods for garment features. CatVTON removes all additional image encoders.

entities.name: VITON-HD Dataset
entities.description: 13,679 upper-body clothing pairs, 11,647/2,032 train/test, 1024×768. Standard VTON benchmark (Choi et al., 2021).

entities.name: DressCode Dataset
entities.description: 48,392/5,400 train/test, full-body + upper/lower/dress garments. DensePose+SCHP masks (Morelli et al., 2022).

entities.name: DeepFashion Dataset
entities.description: 52,712 high-res person images, 13,098/1,896 for garment transfer. In-shop retrieval (Ge et al., 2019).

entities.name: Image-Based Virtual Try-On
entities.description: Composite image of person wearing specified garment while preserving identity, pose, and garment texture detail.

entities.name: Subject-Driven Image Generation
entities.description: Integrating target subject into new scenes with visual consistency. Broader task encompassing VTON, face stylization, object insertion.

entities.name: SSIM
entities.description: Structural Similarity Index (Wang et al., 2004). Paired; higher better.

entities.name: LPIPS
entities.description: Learned Perceptual Image Patch Similarity (Zhang et al., 2018). Deep-feature distance. Paired; lower better.

entities.name: FID
entities.description: Fréchet Inception Distance (Seitzer, 2020). Feature distribution distance. Lower better.

entities.name: KID
entities.description: Kernel Inception Distance (Bińkowski et al., 2021). MMD-based distribution distance. Lower better.

entities.name: OOTDiffusion
entities.description: Novel LDM-based VTON. Outfitting UNet learns garment features single-step. Outfitting Fusion aligns garment+human in self-attn layers without warping. Outfitting Dropout enables CFG-based garment controllability.

entities.name: Outfitting UNet
entities.description: Single-step garment feature extractor parallel to denoising UNet. Encoded garment E(g) processed once to learn multi-scale detail features without iterative denoising.

entities.name: Outfitting Fusion
entities.description: Merges garment features into denoising UNet self-attention layers without explicit warping. Garment+human aligned in shared attention space, bypassing CLIP bottleneck.

entities.name: Outfitting Dropout
entities.description: Randomly drops garment latents during training to enable CFG with respect to garment features. Guidance scale adjusts control strength at inference.

entities.name: Latent Diffusion Models (LDM)
entities.description: Diffusion in VAE-compressed latent space. UNet trained: ||ϵ-ϵ_θ(z_t,t,τ_θ(y))||². OOTDiffusion extends Stable Diffusion LDM (Rombach et al., 2022).

entities.name: Variational Autoencoder (VAE)
entities.description: Encoder E: image→R^{4×h×w} (8× down). Decoder D: latent→image. Standard LDM component encoded by OOTDiffusion for masked human and garment.

entities.name: Denoising UNet
entities.description: UNet ϵ_θ predicting added noise with MSE objective. Alternating ResNet+transformer blocks. OOTDiffusion expands first conv to 8 input channels. Cross-attention for CLIP conditioning.

entities.name: Self-Attention Feature Alignment
entities.description: Garment and noisy human latent interact in denoising UNet's self-attention layers. More direct than CLIP bottleneck or cross-attention injection. Core mechanism of Outfitting Fusion.

entities.name: Classifier-Free Guidance (CFG)
entities.description: ε = ε_uncond + s·(ε_cond − ε_uncond). Balances conditional and unconditional predictions via guidance scale s. OOTDiffusion enables garment-aware CFG through outfitting dropout (Ho & Salimans, 2022).

entities.name: Cross-Attention Conditioning
entities.description: Standard LDM mechanism: CLIP text/image embeddings interact with UNet features via cross-attention layers. OOTDiffusion uses for auxiliary garment label and CLIP textual inversion embeddings.

entities.name: CLIP Textual Inversion
entities.description: Maps visual garment features into CLIP token space for text-conditioned generation (Gal et al., 2022). OOTDiffusion uses as auxiliary input — earlier methods relied solely on this and lost fine details.

entities.name: MSE Diffusion Loss
entities.description: L = E[||ϵ − ϵ_θ(z_t, t, τ_θ(y))||²]. Standard LDM training objective minimizing noise prediction error.

entities.name: ControlNet
entities.description: Adds spatial conditioning blocks to frozen diffusion models (Zhang et al., 2023). Referenced as alternative controllable gen approach.

entities.name: IP-Adapter
entities.description: Decoupled cross-attention for text+image features enabling controllable gen with image prompts (Ye et al., 2023).

entities.name: Stable Diffusion
entities.description: Pretrained latent diffusion model (Rombach et al., 2022). OOTDiffusion initializes from SD weights, leveraging pretrained priors for realistic try-on generation.

entities.name: CLIP Vision Encoder
entities.description: CLIP image encoder (Radford et al., 2021). OOTDiffusion uses for auxiliary garment conditioning via cross-attention. Main garment features bypass CLIP through Outfitting UNet.

entities.name: CLIP Text Encoder
entities.description: CLIP text encoder τ_θ for encoding garment labels. Standard SD component used by OOTDiffusion for auxiliary conditioning.

entities.name: OpenPose
entities.description: Human pose keypoint estimation tool used by OOTDiffusion to generate masked human images for garment region identification.

entities.name: VITON-HD Dataset
entities.description: 13,679 upper-body clothing pairs, 11,647/2,032 train/test, 1024×768. Standard VTON benchmark (Choi et al., 2021).

entities.name: DressCode Dataset
entities.description: Full-body person images with upper/lower/dress garments (Morelli et al., 2022). OOTDiffusion trains separate models per dataset.

entities.name: Image-Based Virtual Try-On
entities.description: Generating outfitted image of target human wearing given garment. Must preserve garment texture/details and human identity/pose.

entities.name: SSIM
entities.description: Structural Similarity Index (Wang et al., 2004). Paired; higher better.

entities.name: LPIPS
entities.description: Learned Perceptual Image Patch Similarity (Zhang et al., 2018). Paired; lower better.

entities.name: FID
entities.description: Frechet Inception Distance (Seitzer, 2020). Feature distribution distance. Lower better.

entities.name: KID
entities.description: Kernel Inception Distance (Binkowski et al., 2021). MMD-based. Lower better.

entities.name: Garment Detail Preservation
entities.description: Critical VTON requirement: retaining garment text, texture, color, pattern, line through generation. OOTDiffusion achieves via self-attention fusion without CLIP bottleneck.

entities.name: Controllable VTON
entities.description: Ability to adjust garment feature influence on try-on result via CFG guidance scale at inference time. Higher scale = stronger garment detail preservation.

entities.name: Texture-Preserving Diffusion Models for High-Fidel

entities.name: Latent Diffusion Models (LDM)
entities.description: Diffusion in VAE-compressed latent space. UNet denoiser trained with MSE loss. (Rombach et al., 2022)

entities.name: Stable Diffusion
entities.description: Pretrained latent diffusion model used as base for VTON initialization (Rombach et al., 2022).

entities.name: Variational Autoencoder (VAE)
entities.description: Encoder: image→latent z (8× down). Decoder: z→image. Standard LDM component.

entities.name: Denoising UNet
entities.description: UNet ϵ_θ predicting added noise. Alternating ResNet blocks (local features) + transformer blocks (global self-attention). Core diffusion backbone.

entities.name: Self-Attention for Diffusion
entities.description: Self-attention layers in UNet transformer blocks enabling global interaction between spatial positions. Critical for garment-person feature fusion in VTON.

entities.name: Cross-Attention Conditioning
entities.description: Standard LDM mechanism injecting text/image embeddings into UNet features via cross-attention layers.

entities.name: ControlNet
entities.description: Adds spatial conditioning control to frozen diffusion models (Zhang et al., 2023).

entities.name: CLIP
entities.description: Contrastive Language-Image Pretraining (Radford et al., 2021). Used for garment feature extraction and text conditioning in VTON.

entities.name: Denoising Diffusion Probabilistic Models (DDPM)
entities.description: Generative framework reversing gradual noising: z_T∼N(0,1) → z_{t-1} = UNet(z_t, t, conditions). T-step iterative denoising. Standard diffusion formulation.

entities.name: DDIM Sampling
entities.description: Denoising Diffusion Implicit Models — deterministic sampling variant of DDPM with fewer steps. Used for faster VTON inference.

entities.name: AdamW Optimizer
entities.description: Adam with decoupled weight decay (Loshchilov & Hutter, 2019). Standard optimizer for diffusion model fine-tuning.

entities.name: Flow Matching
entities.description: Alternative to diffusion: learns vector field transforming simple distribution to data distribution. Enables faster sampling.

entities.name: VITON-HD Dataset
entities.description: 13,679 upper-body clothing pairs, 1024×768 (Choi et al., 2021).

entities.name: DeepFashion Dataset
entities.description: 52,712 high-res person images (Ge et al., 2019).

entities.name: Image-Based Virtual Try-On
entities.description: Generating outfitted image of target human wearing given garment while preserving identity, pose, and garment detail.

entities.name: SSIM
entities.description: Standard image quality metric used for VTON evaluation.

entities.name: LPIPS
entities.description: Standard image quality metric used for VTON evaluation.

entities.name: FID
entities.description: Standard image quality metric used for VTON evaluation.

entities.name: Scaling Laws
entities.description: Empirical power-law relationships predicting loss/performance vs compute, data, or parameter budget. Coined bridge over per-paper scaling-law concepts.

entities.name: Garment-Person Feature Alignment
entities.description: Mechanism aligning garment features with person/pose features for try-on synthesis. Coined bridge over per-paper alignment modules.

entities.name: Extrapolation-Based Iterate Correction
entities.description: Cross-domain primitive: improving a generative process by pushing an iterate beyond its naive update via extrapolation — momentum extrapolates the diffusion sampling trajectory; negative extrapolation pushes away from a self-trained models bias. Coined distant bridge.

claims.text: Divergence artifacts arise from high-order numerical methods with low sampling steps.
claims.verbatim_quote: these methods often produce divergence artifacts, especially with a low number of sampling steps

claims.text: Narrow stability region of high-order methods causes solution divergence, producing artifacts.
claims.verbatim_quote: the narrow stability region of high-order numerical methods can cause solutions to diverge, resulting in divergence artifacts

claims.text: DDIM can be rewritten as an ODE, enabling numerical methods for accelerated sampling.
claims.verbatim_quote: DDIM 1 can be rewritten into an ODE, making it possible to use numerical methods to accelerate the sampling process

claims.text: As order increases, AB stability regions shrink and boundaries become more restrictive.
claims.verbatim_quote: As the order of the method increases, the stability region decreases in size, and its boundary becomes more restrictive

claims.text: Boundary locus technique visualizes stability by solving z=s(θ)=A(e^{-iθ})/B(e^{-iθ}) for θ∈[-π,π].
claims.verbatim_quote: we can obtain the curve z=s(θ)=A(e^{-iθ})/B(e^{-iθ}), where θ∈[-π,π], that represents the boundary of the stability region

claims.text: If δλ falls outside stability region, the numerical solution diverges, manifesting as artifacts with large latent magnitudes.
claims.verbatim_quote: if δλ falls outside the stability region of a numerical method, the numerical solution to u may diverge, resulting in diffusion sampling results with larger magnitudes that later manifest as divergence artifacts

claims.text: When β<1, HB drops to first-order convergence, significantly decreasing image quality.
claims.verbatim_quote: as soon as β deviates from 1, the theoretical order of convergence drops to 1, leading to a significant decrease in image quality

claims.text: β=1 recovers AB2; as β→0, GHVB converges to Euler. GHVB interpolates between adjacent-order AB methods.
claims.verbatim_quote: When β=1, the formulation in Equation 19 is equivalent to the AB2 formulation... As β approaches 0, Equation 17 converges to the 1st-order Euler method

claims.text: HB applied to DPM-Solver++ and PLMS4 with 15 steps significantly reduces artifacts across three text-to-image models.
claims.verbatim_quote: we apply our HB and GHVB techniques to the most popular 2nd and 4th-order solvers, DPM-Solver++ [19] and PLMS4 [20], using 15 sampling steps

claims.text: Applying HB to LTSP consistently produces the lowest FID on pixel-based ADM with classifier guidance.
claims.verbatim_quote: applying our HB momentum to LTSP consistently produces the lowest FID scores

claims.text: GHVB 3.8 and 3.9 mitigate numerical divergence and improve FID vs PLMS4, especially below 10 steps.
claims.verbatim_quote: Our GHVB 3.8 and 3.9 techniques successfully mitigate numerical divergence and lead to improved FID scores compared to PLMS4, particularly when the number of steps is below 10

claims.text: HB 0.8 with PLMS4 can worsen FID vs PLMS4 alone (first-order = same as DDIM), while GHVB maintains higher order.
claims.verbatim_quote: using HB 0.8 with PLMS4 can worsen FID scores compared to using PLMS4 alone, since the method has 1st-order convergence, which is the same as DDIM

claims.text: For GHVB 2.5/3.5, error may be too small at high step counts for reliable convergence verification.
claims.verbatim_quote: for GHVB 2.5 and GHVB 3.5, the estimated error e may be too small when tested with large numbers of steps, and other sources of error may hinder their convergence

claims.text: Momentum techniques are not mutually exclusive with distillation, consistency models, or SDE methods.
claims.verbatim_quote: These techniques are not mutually exclusive

claims.text: DPM-Solver++ uses Equation 3 form which keeps differentiation bounded within pixel range, outperforming Equation 2.
claims.verbatim_quote: Recent research on DPM-Solver++ [27] has shown that Equation 3 outperforms Equation 2 in many cases

claims.text: LTSP divides guided ODE into subproblems but applies Euler to the guidance equation to avoid instability.
claims.verbatim_quote: Lie-Trotter Splitting (LTSP) [26]. This method divides Equation 6 into two subproblems... but can only apply high-order numerical methods to the first equation while resorting to the Euler method for the second equation

claims.text: HB momentum applied to DPM-Solver++ and LTSP effectively improves FID on ADM with classifier guidance.
claims.verbatim_quote: Our techniques effectively improve FID scores for both DPM-Solver++ and LTSP, as shown in Figure 9

claims.text: Countless unexplored ways exist to expand numerical method stability regions beyond HB and GHVB.
claims.verbatim_quote: countless different ways to expand the stability region of a numerical method, which offer many new research opportunities

claims.text: HB momentum expands stability regions, reducing divergence artifacts in diffusion sampling.
claims.verbatim_quote: incorporation of Heavy Ball (HB) momentum... into existing diffusion numerical methods to expand their stability regions

claims.text: GHVB constructs a high-order method with variable accuracy-artifact suppression trade-off.
claims.verbatim_quote: Generalized Heavy Ball (GHVB), constructs a new high-order method that offers a variable trade-off between accuracy and artifact suppression

claims.text: HB applies an exponential moving average to the high-order estimate of existing AB/PLMS methods.
claims.verbatim_quote: Consider a high-order method of the form xn+1 = x^n + δ P^k i=0 bif(xn−i). We can apply HB to it

claims.text: GHVB applies momentum filter before summing high-order terms, generalizing HB to higher orders.
claims.verbatim_quote: The main difference between HB and GHVB is that HB calculates the moving average after summing high-order coefficients, whereas GHVB calculates it before the summation

claims.text: HB momentum adapts Polyak's optimization algorithm for diffusion sampling numerical methods.
claims.verbatim_quote: Polyak's Heavy Ball Momentum [21] is an optimization algorithm that enhances gradient descent... We can apply HB to the Euler method

claims.text: Divergence artifacts are measured by the magnitude score — sum of max-pooled latent magnitudes exceeding threshold.
claims.verbatim_quote: adding HB momentum to PLMS4 [20] or DPM-Solver++[27] can reduce their magnitude scores

claims.text: DNO formulates motion tasks as z*=argmin_z L(f(z)) where z=x_T, optimizing in diffusion latent space rather than raw motion space.
claims.verbatim_quote: z* = arg min_z L(f(z))... our choice of the latent variable is the diffusion's noise at time T, z = x_T

claims.text: DNO optimizes x_T via gradient descent through the ODE solver: solves ODE forward, computes L(x), backpropagates to get ∇_{x_T}L(x).
claims.verbatim_quote: Starting from a certain noise x_T, we solve the ODE, arrive at a prediction x, and evaluate the criterion function L(x). Then, we obtain the gradient ∇_{x_T}L(x) by backpropagating through the ODE solver.

claims.text: Gradients via backprop through ODE have norms spanning multiple orders of magnitude; normalizing to unit norm optimizes faster.
claims.verbatim_quote: gradients via backpropagation through the ODE solver have norms spanning multiple orders of magnitude, making the optimization unstable and slow. We instead propose to normalize the gradient to have a unit norm

claims.text: To preserve original motion during editing, DNO inverts reference motion via DDIM inversion and penalizes distance to the reference noise.
claims.verbatim_quote: we invert the reference motion x_ref using diffusion inversion to obtain the corresponding noise sample x_T_ref... we penalize the distance between x_T_ref and x_T to ensure that it remains close to the reference motion

claims.text: Decorrelation loss across temporal resolutions prevents foot skating by discouraging correlated latent samples that produce poor motions.
claims.verbatim_quote: regularizing the noise to decorrelate each latent dimension alleviates the foot skating problem... L_decorr significantly improves the Foot skating ratio from 0.10 to 0.07 and the Jitter from 0.40 to 0.33

claims.text: DNO computes criteria on exact x after full denoising, unlike guided diffusion which computes on expected E[x_0|x_t] with approximation error.
claims.verbatim_quote: For guided diffusion methods, the criterion is computed on an expected x̂=E[x_0|x_t]... resulting in severe error when Var[x_0|x_t] is large. For DNO, the criterion is exactly computed on x after the full-chain denoising

claims.text: DNO-MDM outperforms GMD on motion editing across all actions in content preservation and objective error, with significantly lower Jitter.
claims.verbatim_quote: our proposed DNO outperforms GMD [33], the state of the art in spatially-conditioned motion generation, in all actions and metrics, except for Foot skating ratio on crawling

claims.text: DNO achieves significantly lower Jitter than GMD, producing smoother motions after editing.
claims.verbatim_quote: achieves significantly lower Jitter

claims.text: DNO-GMD combination outperforms DNO-MDM, DNO-MLD, and the optimization-based HuMoR on motion refinement.
claims.verbatim_quote: The DNO-GMD combination outperforms the weaker combinations, DNO-MLD and DNO-MDM, and the optimization-based motion prior HuMoR

claims.text: DNO is plug-and-play and model-agnostic, working with any trained motion diffusion model including MDM, MLD, and GMD.
claims.verbatim_quote: DNO is plug-and-play and model-agnostic, it works with any trained motion diffusion model. When paired with MDM, which is trained on the HumanML3D dataset, we name the combination DNO-MDM

claims.text: The latent decorrelation loss reduces foot skating ratio from 0.10 to 0.07 and Jitter from 0.40 to 0.33.
claims.verbatim_quote: L_decorr significantly improves the Foot skating ratio from 0.10 to 0.07 and the Jitter from 0.40 to 0.33

claims.text: Normalized gradients improve MPJPE from 30.2 to 8.7 cm, demonstrating that gradient normalization is critical for convergence.
claims.verbatim_quote: our choice of using normalized gradients has improved the solutions by reducing MPJPE (which corresponds to the lower Lpose value) from 30.2 to 8.7 cm

claims.text: DNO-GMD outperforms the guided diffusion method GMD in motion refinement, which struggles with fine pose signals.
claims.verbatim_quote: any DNO combination outperforms the guided diffusion method, GMD, in the motion refinement task. For the GMD baseline... it struggles to satisfy fine pose signals

claims.text: DiffusionLight leverages SDXL trained on billions of standard images to render a chrome ball for lighting estimation.
claims.verbatim_quote: we leverage diffusion models trained on billions of standard images to render a chrome ball into the input image

claims.text: Iterative inpainting automatically locates good noise neighborhoods by sample averaging, producing consistent high-quality chrome balls.
claims.verbatim_quote: we propose a simple algorithm to automatically locate a good noise neighborhood by sample averaging

claims.text: Iterative inpainting leverages the discovery that same initial noise maps produce semantically similar inpainted areas across different input images.
claims.verbatim_quote: The same initial noise map leads to the generation semantically similar inpainted areas regardless of the input image

claims.text: Iterative inpainting applies SDEdit to the median ball composite: adds noise to t=0.8T then denoises, repeating for 2 iterations.
claims.verbatim_quote: we apply SDEdit [41] by adding noise to B to simulate the diffusion process up to time step t... we can repeat the process by using SDEdit to generate another set of N chrome balls

claims.text: Exposure bracketing LoRA steers sampling to produce chrome balls with specific exposure compensation values for HDR merging.
claims.verbatim_quote: our idea is to use the exposure bracketing technique by inpainting multiple chrome balls with different exposure values and combining them to produce a linearized HDR output

claims.text: A single LoRA is trained (not multiple per EV) to preserve overall scene structure across exposures through weight sharing.
claims.verbatim_quote: We choose to train a single LoRA as opposed to multiple LoRAs for individual EVs because it helps preserve the overall scene structure across exposures due to weight sharing

claims.text: Luminance replacement merges HDR by replacing overexposed luminance values with exposure-corrected values from lower EVs while retaining chroma from EV0.
claims.verbatim_quote: we can merge the luminances while retaining the chroma from the normally exposed EV0 image to reduce ghosting

claims.text: Depth-conditioned ControlNet with a white circle painted at the center enables reliable chrome ball insertion instead of content reconstruction.
claims.verbatim_quote: we paint a circle both at the depth map's center with the distance closest to the camera (visualized as white) and in an inpaint mask

claims.text: DiffusionLight outperforms StyleLight in Angular Error: 49.5% lower for diffuse, 27.8% for matte, 12.4% for mirror spheres on Laval Indoor.
claims.verbatim_quote: our method outperforms StyleLight in terms of Angular Error and Normalized RMSE on Laval indoor dataset, with significantly lower Angular Error: 49.5% (diffuse), 27.8% (matte), and 12.4% (mirror)

claims.text: DiffusionLight is competitive with StyleLight on si-RMSE and achieves better performance on two out of three metrics across both datasets.
claims.verbatim_quote: our method outperforms StyleLight in... Normalized RMSE on Laval indoor dataset

claims.text: DiffusionLight generalizes to in-the-wild images beyond benchmarks where StyleLight's performance drops due to limited training panorama diversity.
claims.verbatim_quote: StyleLight's performance drops with a large 39.7% gap in Angular Error for mirror spheres [on Poly Haven outdoor scenes]

claims.text: DiffSDA models static and dynamic factors as interdependent, unlike prior work which assumes independence, improving expressivity by 13%.
claims.verbatim_quote: our static and dynamic factors are interdependent... adopting dependent modeling improves generation quality by 13%

claims.text: The sequential semantic encoder uses modality-specific backbone + LSTM to extract static factor s_0 from last hidden state and dynamic factors d_0^{1:V} from all hiddens.
claims.verbatim_quote: it consists of a U-Net for video data and an MLP for other modalities, coupled with linear layers... an LSTM module summarizes the sequence into a latent representation h^{1:V}

claims.text: The decoder follows EDM framework with only 63 NFEs during inference, taking noisy input and disentangled factors to return denoised estimate via preconditioning.
claims.verbatim_quote: we follow the decoding in EDM (Karras et al., 2022), featuring only 63 neural function evaluations (NFEs) during inference

claims.text: DiffSDA uses a single standard diffusion loss term, unlike prior methods requiring 5+ hyperparameters for loss balancing, while still achieving disentanglement.
claims.verbatim_quote: our method is based on a single standard diffusion loss term, while producing high-quality results

claims.text: DiffSDA produces high-quality swaps preserving fine details including head angle, mouth/eye orientation while transferring dynamics, where SPYL struggles with both reconstruction and swap.
claims.verbatim_quote: while the people in these sequences are very different, many fine details are transferred, including head angle and orientation, as well as mouth and eyes orientation and openness

claims.text: DiffSDA outperforms SPYL and DBSE on AED across all datasets except TaiChi-HD where it achieves second best.
claims.verbatim_quote: DiffSDA outperforms SOTA previous (SPYL, DBSE) approaches across all datasets, except for AED on TaiChi-HD, where we attain the second best error

claims.text: DiffSDA's AKD errors are significantly lower than SPYL and DBSE, demonstrating superior dynamic preservation in swaps.
claims.verbatim_quote: our AKD errors are significantly lower than SPYL and DBSE

claims.text: DiffSDA trained on VoxCeleb successfully performs conditional swap on unseen MUG/CelebV-HQ, adding necessary body pose details beyond training distribution.
claims.verbatim_quote: DiffSDA also adds the necessary details to mimic the body pose. We emphasize that the MUG dataset does not include sequences similar to the third row

claims.text: PCA on sampled static vectors reveals principal components controlling interpretable attributes: positive direction = more masculine, negative = more feminine, while preserving dynamics.
claims.verbatim_quote: traversing in the positive direction yields more masculine appearances, and in contrast, going in the negative direction produces more feminine characters

claims.text: DiffSDA achieves disentanglement gap of 42.29% on TIMIT vs 31.11% by DBSE, an 11%+ improvement, with low Static EER and high Dynamic EER.
claims.verbatim_quote: Our model, achieves in TIMIT a disentanglement gap improvement of over 11%, with a 42.29% compared to 31.11% achieved by DBSE

claims.text: DiffSDA outperforms SPYL across all time series prediction and classification tasks with single loss optimization vs SPYL's 5 hyperparameters.
claims.verbatim_quote: our model outperforms across all tasks

claims.text: DiffSDA achieves better reconstruction MSE by orders of magnitude vs DBSE and SPYL across all video datasets.
claims.verbatim_quote: DiffSDA to be orders-of-magnitude better than other methods

claims.text: Disentanglement emerges because the static factor is shared across τ (cannot hold dynamics) and dynamic factors are low-dimensional (cannot store static features).
claims.verbatim_quote: the static factor s_0 is shared across τ... thus it will not hold dynamic information, and the dynamic factors d_0^τ are low-dimensional, making it difficult for d_0^τ to store static features

claims.text: HB momentum applied to PLMS4 with 15 sampling steps significantly reduces divergence artifacts in text-to-image generation.
claims.verbatim_quote: we apply our HB and GHVB techniques to the most popular 2nd and 4th-order solvers, DPM-Solver++ [19] and PLMS4 [20], using 15 sampling steps

claims.text: Applying HB momentum to LTSP consistently produces the lowest FID scores in pixel-based classifier-guided sampling.
claims.verbatim_quote: applying our HB momentum to LTSP consistently produces the lowest FID scores

claims.text: DNO supports diverse editing modes through composable loss functions: L_pose for trajectories, L_obs for obstacle SDF, L_cont for content preservation.
claims.verbatim_quote: DNO supports any use cases where criteria can be defined as a function of motion

claims.text: MDM uses the relative-root representation (263 features) abstracting absolute location for better generalization.
claims.verbatim_quote: The relative-root representation [17] has been widely adopted for text-to-motion diffusion models [73]

claims.text: Neon performs negative extrapolation away from degraded weights
claims.verbatim_quote: we perform negative extrapolation: θ_Neon = θ_r - w(θ_s - θ_r) = (1 + w)θ_r - wθ_s, where w controls the extrapolation strength

claims.text: Naive self-training leads to model autophagy disorder (MAD/model collapse)
claims.verbatim_quote: such naïve self-training has been shown to lead to 'model autophagy disorder' (MAD) or model collapse

claims.text: The key insight is that degradation from self-training is anti-aligned with the real-data population gradient, enabling Neon
claims.verbatim_quote: our key insight is that the degradation due to self-training is not random noise but rather a power signal that is anti-aligned with the real-data population gradient

claims.text: Neon exploits gradient anti-alignment through a simple parameter merge
claims.verbatim_quote: Neon (Negative Extrapolation from self-traiNing) exploits this anti-alignment through a simple parameter merge

claims.text: Neon achieves state-of-the-art FID of 1.02 on ImageNet-256 with 0.36% additional compute
claims.verbatim_quote: Neon elevates the xAR-L model to a new state-of-the-art FID of 1.02 with only 0.36% additional training compute

claims.text: Neon elevates xAR-L from FID 1.28 to 1.02 on ImageNet-256
claims.verbatim_quote: Neon elevates xAR-L from an FID of 1.28 to the state-of-the-art 1.02 using only 0.36% additional compute

claims.text: Neon works across diffusion, flow matching, autoregressive, and IMM models
claims.verbatim_quote: We demonstrate Neon's universality across a range of architectures (diffusion, flow matching, autoregressive, and inductive moment matching models)

claims.text: Mode-seeking inference samplers create predictable anti-alignment between synthetic and population gradients
claims.verbatim_quote: mode-seeking inference samplers create a predictable anti-alignment between the synthetic and population gradients that guarantees the effectiveness of negative extrapolation

claims.text: Neon's improvement mechanism operates through a precision-recall trade-off
claims.verbatim_quote: Neon's improvement mechanism operates through a precision-recall trade-off that redistributes probability mass from over- to under-represented modes

claims.text: The Neon degradation signal is transferable across different model architectures
claims.verbatim_quote: Neon is transferable across different model architectures. We confirm this empirically in Figure 8, by improving a baseline unconditional EDM-VP model

claims.text: Very small synthetic datasets are variance-limited, very large ones amplify curvature effects
claims.verbatim_quote: Very small sets are variance-limited, very large sets amplify curvature effects (inflating the quadratic term in our Taylor expansion), while moderate sizes optimally balance these competing factors.

claims.text: Neon requires no auxiliary models, no inference modifications, no likelihood computations
claims.verbatim_quote: In contrast to existing methods for synthetic data augmentation, Neon requires no additional real training data, no access to the original training data, no auxiliary models, no likelihood computation, and no inference modifications

claims.text: Can diversity-promoting samplers foster positive alignment between synthetic and real data, enabling direct self-improvement without inversion?
claims.verbatim_quote: Can we identify diversity-promoting samplers that foster positive alignment between synthetic and real data, thereby enabling direct self-improvement without inversion?

claims.text: Can we actively synthesize optimal bad datasets that maximize the corrective signal?
claims.verbatim_quote: Can we actively synthesize optimal 'bad' datasets that elicit a degradation direction that maximizes the corrective signal?

claims.text: Regularized parameter scaling with 30x larger weight decay enables monotone power-law scaling under data constraints
claims.verbatim_quote: We show that to get the best performance from these over-parameterized, epoched models, it is critical to regularize pre-training with much higher weight decay than standard practice

claims.text: Loss asymptote evaluation measures best possible performance via the limit of the scaling law as N approaches infinity
claims.verbatim_quote: we propose evaluating monotone scaling recipes by the asymptote of their scaling law (e.g. 3.43 for the regularized recipe)

claims.text: Ensembling recipe achieves a lower loss asymptote than regularized parameter scaling as K approaches infinity
claims.verbatim_quote: The ensembling recipe achieves a lower asymptote as K → ∞ compared to the regularized recipe as N → ∞

claims.text: Joint scaling composing parameter and ensemble scaling achieves the lowest asymptote, requiring 5.17x less data
claims.verbatim_quote: our best intervention combining epoching, regularization, parameter scaling, and ensemble scaling achieves an asymptote at 200M tokens using 5.17× less data than our baseline

claims.text: Optimal weight decay is 30x larger than standard practice for over-parameterized models
claims.verbatim_quote: We find this amount to be inadequate for preventing overfitting under data constraints as the optimal weight decay is 30× larger than standard practice

claims.text: The exponent of 1.02 for parameter scaling is much higher than Chinchilla's 0.34
claims.verbatim_quote: The exponent of 1.02 for parameter scaling is high given that Chinchilla finds a parameter scaling exponent of 0.34

claims.text: Ensembling independently trained models achieves a lower loss asymptote than parameter scaling
claims.verbatim_quote: we consider ensembling: independently train K models and average their logits for generation

claims.text: Joint scaling composing parameter and ensemble scaling achieves the lowest asymptote
claims.verbatim_quote: Composing parameter scaling (N→∞) and ensemble scaling (K→∞): both the number and size of models increase

claims.text: Distilling an 8-ensemble into a single 300M model retains 83% of ensembling benefit
claims.verbatim_quote: Distilling an 8-ensemble into a single 300M student retains 83% of the ensembling benefit

claims.text: Data scaling laws are expected to be noisy, but predict recipes decay at similar rates
claims.verbatim_quote: Although the data scaling laws are expected to be noisy, they predict that all recipes decay at a similar rate with exponents between 0.23 and 0.24

claims.text: Self-distillation with a 300M teacher and 300M student outperforms the best regularized 300M model
claims.verbatim_quote: using a 300M model as a teacher results in a 300M student model that outperforms the best regularized 300M model

claims.text: Validation loss improvements translate to downstream benchmarks with 9% improvement
claims.verbatim_quote: our best ensemble outperforms our best unregularized model by 9% on average over PIQA, SciQ, and ARC Easy

claims.text: GLASS Flows construct an inner flow matching model to sample from p_{t'|t}, obtainable from pre-trained models without fine-tuning
claims.verbatim_quote: GLASS Flows construct an 'inner flow matching model' to sample from p_{t'|t}...easily obtained from pre-trained flow matching models without any fine-tuning

claims.text: The sufficient statistic transformation enables extracting the inner flow model from pre-trained weights
claims.verbatim_quote: this transformation relies on the concept of a sufficient statistic, a fundamental tool in theoretical statistics

claims.text: GLASS Flows sample Markov transitions with significantly higher efficiency and lower discretization error than SDEs
claims.verbatim_quote: GLASS Flows can sample Markov transitions with significantly higher efficiency and lower discretization error than SDEs

claims.text: GLASS Flows combined with Feynman-Kac Steering improve state-of-the-art text-to-image generation
claims.verbatim_quote: Combined with Feynman-Kac Steering, GLASS Flows improve state-of-the-art performance in text-to-image generation

claims.text: Text-to-image generation via GLASS performs on par with ODE sampling, eliminating the efficiency-stochasticity trade-off
claims.verbatim_quote: Text-to-image generation via GLASS Flows is shown to perform on par with ODE sampling, indicating GLASS Flows have eliminated the efficiency and stochasticity tradeoff

claims.text: The optimal choice of correlation parameter rho depends on the data and model
claims.verbatim_quote: the optimal choice of ρ is dependent on the data and model and may well differ for other settings

claims.text: GLASS is a novel sampling scheme resulting in Markov chains preserving the marginals of pre-trained models
claims.verbatim_quote: GLASS Flows serve as a simple, drop-in solution for inference-time scaling of flow and diffusion models

claims.text: DDPM transitions are a special case of GLASS transitions with specific correlation parameter rho
claims.verbatim_quote: DDPM transitions are a special case of GLASS transitions

claims.text: GLASS Flows achieve significantly better FID than DDPM for posterior sampling with the same number of steps
claims.verbatim_quote: GLASS Flows achieve significantly better FID than DDPM sampling for the same number of sampling steps

claims.text: One could explore applying GLASS Flows to other methods relying on SDE sampling, e.g. reward fine-tuning or image editing
claims.verbatim_quote: one could explore applying GLASS Flows to other methods relying on SDE sampling, e.g. some reward fine-tuning or image editing methods

claims.text: One could explore learning or dynamically adjusting the correlation parameter rho defining the GLASS transitions
claims.verbatim_quote: one could explore learning or dynamically adjusting the correlation parameter ρ defining the GLASS transitions

claims.text: Many algorithms require to sample Markov transitions via SDE sampling, which is significantly less efficient and often less performant than ODE sampling
claims.verbatim_quote: many algorithms require to sample Markov transitions via SDE sampling, which is significantly less efficient and often less performant than ODE sampling

claims.text: Knowledge graphs accumulate explicit knowledge that can be written down
claims.verbatim_quote: While many definitions for *knowledge* have been proposed, we refer to what Nonaka and Takeuchi [94] call "*explicit knowledge*," i.e., something that is known and can be written down.

claims.text: Enterprise KGs are internal to a company for commercial use-cases
claims.verbatim_quote: Enterprise knowledge graphs are typically internal to a company and applied for commercial use-cases [95].

claims.text: Open KGs are published online for public good
claims.verbatim_quote: Open knowledge graphs are published online, making their content accessible for the public good.

claims.text: Shapes graphs validate that data graphs follow a particular structure
claims.verbatim_quote: One mechanism to facilitate such validation is to use *shapes graphs*.

claims.text: TransE is a seminal translational approach for knowledge graph embeddings
claims.verbatim_quote: A seminal approach is TransE [17].

claims.text: SME is an early neural model using non-linear scoring functions

claims.text: OWL 2 DL is a restricted fragment with decidable entailment

claims.text: Embeddings suffer from the out-of-vocabulary problem for unseen nodes
claims.verbatim_quote: Embeddings further suffer from the *out-of-vocabulary* problem, where they are often unable to provide results for inputs involving previously unseen nodes or edge-labels.

claims.text: Ontologies adopt NUNA and OWA as the general case
claims.verbatim_quote: Ontologies typically adopt the NUNA and OWA, i.e., the most general case, which considers that data may be incomplete, and two nodes may refer to the same entity.

claims.text: Graph algorithms include centrality analysis to identify important nodes
