claims.text: The article concludes with future research directions for KGs
claims.verbatim_quote: We conclude with high-level future research directions for knowledge graphs.

claims.text: Heterogeneous graphs have node types as part of the model unlike del graphs
claims.verbatim_quote: A heterogeneous graph [61, 142, 154] (or *heterogeneous information network* [128, 129]) is a graph where each node and edge is assigned one type.

claims.text: Joint verification faces intractability: computing full joint probabilities over all possible decoding paths is computationally infeasible
claims.verbatim_quote: simply applying the resampling strategy used in tokenwise verification would require full joint probabilities over all possible decoding paths to correctly recover the target distribution, which is computationally infeasible

claims.text: Generalized divergence measures total deficient mass within a subset Ω'
claims.verbatim_quote: The generalized divergence D_Ω'(p,q) measures the total deficient mass, i.e., how much probability mass is missing in the draft q relative to the target p within the subset Ω'

claims.text: A target distribution over subset Ω' can be fully recovered via resampling iff D_Ω'(p,q) ≤ D_Ω'(q,p)
claims.verbatim_quote: A target distribution over Ω' ⊆ Ω can be fully recovered via resampling iff DΩ'(p,q) ≤ DΩ'(q,p)

claims.text: Integrating HSD into EAGLE-3 yields over 12% performance gain, establishing state-of-the-art decoding efficiency
claims.verbatim_quote: Integrating HSD into EAGLE-3 yields over a 12% performance gain, establishing state-of-the-art decoding efficiency without compromising distribution fidelity

claims.text: HSD yields an average 6.7% improvement in decoding speed across diverse benchmarks
claims.verbatim_quote: HSD delivers a practical advance in inference scaling, achieving an average 6.7% improvement in decoding speed across diverse benchmarks and model sizes while preserving distributional fidelity

claims.text: Branch divergence captures how much probability mass is missing locally in a branch
claims.verbatim_quote: Branch divergence captures how much probability mass is missing locally

claims.text: Capped Branch Resampling removes the overhead of additional target model calls, enabling recovery with a single resampling step
claims.verbatim_quote: To remove this overhead, we introduce Capped Branch Resampling, yielding our final Hierarchical Speculative Decoding (HSD)

claims.text: Blockwise Verification falls short of ideal acceptance rates and has unclear compatibility with multi-draft methods
claims.verbatim_quote: Blockwise Verification still falls short of the ideal case, and both its underlying mechanism and compatibility with other methods remain unclear

claims.text: HSD is a lossless, explainable verification method orthogonal to drafting, integrable with existing frameworks
claims.verbatim_quote: HSD is a lossless, explainable verification method orthogonal to drafting, integrable with existing frameworks, establishing SOTA decoding efficiency

claims.text: HSD is a provably lossless verification method that ensures the decoded tokens are statistically indistinguishable from the target model
claims.verbatim_quote: HSD is a provably lossless verification method that significantly boosts the expected number of accepted tokens and overcomes joint intractability

claims.text: HSD's design easily combines with other approaches, such as multi-draft setups
claims.verbatim_quote: our method is designed to easily combine with other approaches, such as the widely adopted multi-draft setups

claims.text: In tokenwise speculative decoding, each token is drafted from q(x_t) and verified against p(x_t)
claims.verbatim_quote: In tokenwise speculative sampling, each token x_t is drafted from q(x_t) and verified against p(x_t)

claims.text: Verification is a key bottleneck in improving inference speed while maintaining distribution fidelity
claims.verbatim_quote: Verification is a key bottleneck in improving inference speed while maintaining distribution fidelity in Speculative Decoding

claims.text: HSD provides strong explainability and generality making it integrable into a wide range of speculative decoding frameworks
claims.verbatim_quote: its strong explainability and generality make it readily integrable into a wide range of speculative decoding frameworks

claims.text: RDF is a standard data model based on del graphs
claims.verbatim_quote: A standard data model based on del graphs is the **Resource Description Framework (RDF)** [24].

claims.text: Full entailment for all features is undecidable but practical algorithms exist
claims.verbatim_quote: Given two graphs, deciding if the first entails the second—per all of the features in Tables [2–4] is *undecidable*: No (finite) algorithm for such entailment can exist that halts on all inputs with the correct true/false answer [53].

claims.text: Rule mining discovers rules from positive and negative edges
claims.verbatim_quote: The goal of rule mining is to identify new rules that entail a high ratio of positive edges from other positive edges, but entail a low ratio of negative edges from positive edges.

claims.text: The modern incarnation stems from the 2012 Google Knowledge Graph announcement
claims.verbatim_quote: the modern incarnation of the phrase stems from the 2012 announcement of the Google Knowledge Graph [122]

claims.text: GraphRAG combines knowledge graph generation and QFS to support human sensemaking over entire text corpora
claims.verbatim_quote: We have presented GraphRAG, a RAG approach that combines knowledge graph generation and query-focused summarization (QFS) to support human sensemaking over entire text corpora.

claims.text: Vector RAG fails on global sensemaking questions since they require QFS, not explicit retrieval
claims.verbatim_quote: However, RAG fails on global questions directed at an entire text corpus, such as "What are the main themes in the dataset?", since this is inherently a queryfocused summarization (QFS) task, rather than an explicit retrieval task.

claims.text: GraphRAG achieves substantial improvements over vector RAG for comprehensiveness of answers
claims.verbatim_quote: Initial evaluations show substantial improvements over a vector RAG baseline for both the comprehensiveness and diversity of answers

claims.text: GraphRAG achieves substantial improvements over vector RAG for diversity of answers
claims.verbatim_quote: Initial evaluations show substantial improvements over a vector RAG baseline for both the comprehensiveness and diversity of answers

claims.text: GraphRAG uses Leiden community detection hierarchically to partition the graph into communities
claims.verbatim_quote: we use Leiden community detection [Traag et al., 2019] in a hierarchical manner, recursively detecting sub-communities within each detected community until reaching leaf communities that can no longer be partitioned.

claims.text: GraphRAG answers queries through map-reduce processing of community summaries
claims.verbatim_quote: GraphRAG answers queries through map-reduce processing of community summaries; in the map step, the summaries are used to provide partial answers to the query independently and in parallel, then in the reduce step, the partial answers are combined and used to generate a final global answer.

claims.text: GraphRAG uses LLM-as-a-judge for evaluation against vector RAG
claims.verbatim_quote: To demonstrate this ability, we developed a novel application of the LLM-as-a-judge technique [Zheng et al., 2024] suitable for questions targeting broad issues and themes where there is no ground-truth answer.

claims.text: Self-reflection prompt engineering technique improves quality of knowledge graph extraction
claims.verbatim_quote: The choice of prompt engineering techniques has a strong impact on the quality of knowledge graph extraction [Zhu et al., 2024], and different techniques have different costs in terms of tokens consumed and generated by the model.

claims.text: GraphRAG strongly outperforms vector RAG when using GPT-4 as the LLM
claims.verbatim_quote: Results show GraphRAG strongly outperforms vector RAG when using GPT-4 as the LLM.

claims.text: Community summaries provide global descriptions and insights over the corpus
claims.verbatim_quote: Together, these community summaries provide global descriptions and insights over the corpus.

claims.text: GraphRAG first constructs a knowledge graph where nodes correspond to key entities
claims.verbatim_quote: GraphRAG first uses an LLM to construct a knowledge graph, where nodes correspond to key entities in the corpus and edges represent relationships between those entities.

claims.text: Adaptive benchmarking generates global sensemaking queries for LLM evaluation
claims.verbatim_quote: In this work, we propose an adaptive benchmarking approach to generating global sensemaking queries for the LLM.

claims.text: Empowerment comparisons showed mixed results for global approaches vs vector RAG
claims.verbatim_quote: Empowerment comparisons showed mixed results for both global approaches versus vector RAG (SS) and GraphRAG approaches versus source text summarization (TS).

claims.text: Claim-based measures validate comprehensiveness and diversity results from Experiment 1
claims.verbatim_quote: To validate the comprehensiveness and diversity results from Experiment 1, we implemented claimbased measures of these qualities.

claims.text: Leiden algorithm creates community hierarchy enabling divide-and-conquer global summarization
claims.verbatim_quote: Each level of this hierarchy provides a community partition that covers the nodes of the graph in a mutually exclusive, collectively exhaustive way, enabling divide-and-conquer global summarization.

claims.text: GraphRAG achieved comprehensiveness win rates between 72-83% for Podcast transcripts
claims.verbatim_quote: global approaches achieved comprehensiveness win rates between 72-83% (p<.001) for Podcast transcripts and 72-80% (p<.001) for News articles

claims.text: Vertical KG is the semantic network of what the agent knows
claims.verbatim_quote: The insight: an agentic system needs **two complementary structures**.

claims.text: Horizontal workflow graph is how the agent acts, structured as a DAG
claims.verbatim_quote: **Horizontal workflow graph** — *how the agent acts*. A DAG (directed acyclic graph) of reasoning and execution nodes that structures decisions into inspectable, enforceable steps.

claims.text: Vertical KG is what the agent knows
claims.verbatim_quote: **Vertical knowledge graph** — *what the agent knows*. A semantic network of entities, relationships, constraints, and temporal metadata.

claims.text: Harness design sets the upper bound on agent behavior, not model capability
claims.verbatim_quote: the book's strong claim is that **harness design, not model capability, sets the upper bound on agent behavior.**

claims.text: Context graph is a KG optimized for agent use with three distinguishing ideas
claims.verbatim_quote: A context graph is a KG *optimized for agent use*.

claims.text: Homoiconicity lets agents modify their own operational logic
claims.verbatim_quote: Homoiconicity lets agents **modify their own operational logic**.

claims.text: Ontologies ensure same concept has one representation
claims.verbatim_quote: semantic consistency — same concept, one representation (customer vs client vs patron).

claims.text: RedAI splits vulnerability discovery across scanner and validator nodes distinguished by tool surface
claims.verbatim_quote: Nodes differ by tool surface, not by prompt. The book's sharpest public example is Kyle Polley's **RedAI** (open-sourced April 2026): vulnerability discovery split across two node roles distinguished entirely at the tool level.

claims.text: Event-centric pattern structures knowledge around occurrences enabling temporal reasoning
claims.verbatim_quote: Enables temporal reasoning + causal analysis: what meetings did Alice attend before the project review?

claims.text: Three-graph architecture prevents unverified info from polluting trusted data
claims.verbatim_quote: Merging them into one graph creates four problems: unverified info pollutes trusted data, provenance is lost, extraction errors cascade, and validating the agent's reasoning becomes impossible.

claims.text: ER links subject graph entities to domain graph entities via similarity search
claims.verbatim_quote: Entity resolution linking subject to domain: A review mentions the Stockholm chair. The system extracts a Subject_Product, finds candidate matches in the domain graph via similarity search, scores them.

claims.text: RDF enables native inference while Property Graphs are faster for traversal
claims.verbatim_quote: LPGs (labeled property graphs, e.g. Neo4j) store nodes-with-properties connected by labeled edges — fast, intuitive, great for traversal-heavy work like dependency analysis. RDF represents everything as subject-predicate-object triples with formal logical semantics, enabling native inference.

claims.text: SKOS mappings tie vocabularies together across different systems
claims.verbatim_quote: SKOS (simple knowledge organization system) mappings tie vocabularies together

claims.text: Classical NER requires labeled training data; structured outputs collapse both stages into a single Claude call per document
claims.verbatim_quote: We collapse both stages into a single Claude call per document. The key is **structured outputs**: we define the output shape as a Pydantic model and pass it to `client.messages.parse()`.

claims.text: Serialized subgraphs let Claude answer multi-hop questions with edge-level citations
claims.verbatim_quote: We serialize a relevant subgraph as triples and let Claude reason over it.

claims.text: Hub nodes get rich profiles synthesized across every document that mentioned them
claims.verbatim_quote: hub nodes got rich profiles synthesized across every document that mentioned them, with structured time ranges and traceable key facts.

claims.text: Extraction descriptions are used as disambiguation context for entity resolution
claims.verbatim_quote: The descriptions matter: "Armstrong — first person to walk on the Moon" and "Armstrong — jazz trumpeter" have the same name but should not merge.

claims.text: Traditional string similarity works for typos but fails on different names for the same person like Edwin Aldrin vs Buzz Aldrin
claims.verbatim_quote: That works for typos but fails on "Edwin Aldrin" vs "Buzz Aldrin" — two names with zero character overlap that refer to the same person.

claims.text: Grounded answer is traceable while ungrounded answer draws on pretraining
claims.verbatim_quote: The ungrounded answer draws on Claude's pretraining and may be correct — Apollo 11 is famous. But the grounded answer is **traceable**: every claim cites an edge we extracted from a specific document.

claims.text: KG quality measured with precision and recall against a gold set using the evaluation harness

claims.text: LST consistently improves speech accuracy while also improving text performance, achieving up to +6.5% absolute gain on speech HellaSwag
claims.verbatim_quote: LST consistently improves speech accuracy while also improving text performance, achieving up to +6.5% absolute gain on speech HellaSwag in compute-controlled training (+5.3% in data-controlled training).

claims.text: LST is based on the byte-latent transformer (BLT) architecture
claims.verbatim_quote: LST is based on the byte-latent transformer (BLT) architecture (Pagnoni et al., 2024), comprising an encoder that dynamically groups sequences of speech tokens into higher-level speech patches

claims.verbatim_quote: Curriculum patching gradually transitions from alignment-based to static patching.

claims.text: LST with Curriculum Patching reduces the speech-text performance gap from 9.4 to 6.7

claims.text: LST provides consistent gains in both modalities, with advantages apparent from the earliest scale and amplified as model capacity increases

claims.verbatim_quote: RealUID, a universal distillation framework for all matching models that seamlessly incorporates real data into the distillation procedure without GANs.

claims.text: Since all of the above optimize conditional matching objectives to recover an ODE/SDE for generation, we refer to them collectively as matching models
claims.verbatim_quote: Since all of the above optimize conditional matching objectives to recover an ODE/SDE for generation, we refer to them collectively as matching models.

claims.verbatim_quote: We build a universal distillation framework as a single min-max optimization, implicitly minimizing squared l2-distance between teacher and student functions.

claims.text: GRAND achieves competitive results on many standard graph benchmarks
claims.verbatim_quote: GRAND achieves competitive results on many standard graph benchmarks.

claims.text: GRAND approaches deep learning on graphs as a continuous diffusion process and treats GNNs as discretisations of an underlying PDE
claims.verbatim_quote: Graph Neural Diffusion (GRAND) that approaches deep learning on graphs as a continuous diffusion process

claims.text: The explicit scheme is stable for 0 less than tau less than 1. The implicit scheme is unconditionally stable for any tau greater than 0

claims.verbatim_quote: On ogb-arxiv, our results are slightly inferior to the best-performing GAT, which, however, requires 20 times as many parameters.

claims.text: Standard GNNs are equivalent to the explicit single-step Euler scheme that is inefficient and requires small step sizes
claims.verbatim_quote: Standard GNNs are equivalent to the explicit single-step Euler scheme that is inefficient and requires small step sizes.

claims.text: In future work we intend to overcome these limitations by introducing time-dependent parameters and consider more general nonlinear diffusion equations
claims.verbatim_quote: In future work, we intend to overcome these limitation by introducing a theta = theta(t) as described in (Queiruga et al., 2020; Zhang et al., 2019).

claims.text: The size of the hidden state vector must be constant for all layers and the same set of parameters theta must be used for all layers
claims.verbatim_quote: the size of the hidden state vector must be constant for all layers (a usual situation in GNNs), and second, the same set of parameters theta must be used for all layers.

claims.text: for implicit solvers the benefit of sparsification is independent of the step size

claims.text: DNO uses DDIM-ODE as its deterministic sampler for backpropagation.
claims.verbatim_quote: To obtain a sample based on deterministic sampling, we can solve the associated ODE using an ODE solver starting from x_T

claims.text: Both DNO's normalized gradient descent and HB momentum use momentum-based optimization to stabilize iterative processes.
claims.verbatim_quote: When combined with momentum, the update becomes a moving average of directions, which is robust to gradient norm outliers

claims.text: DiffusionLight uses Stable Diffusion XL, a successor to the Stable Diffusion model used in diffusion sampling experiments.
claims.verbatim_quote: Our key component is based on Stable Diffusion XL [45] with depth-conditioned ControlNet [69]

claims.text: DiffusionLight's iterative inpainting addresses a form of generation inconsistency analogous to divergence artifacts in low-step sampling.
claims.verbatim_quote: The chrome balls often contain undesirable patterns and fail to convincingly reflect environment lighting

claims.text: DiffSDA's EDM-based decoder operates on the same diffusion ODE principles studied in diffusion sampling momentum research.
claims.verbatim_quote: Our decoder follows the decoding in EDM (Karras et al., 2022), featuring only 63 neural function evaluations

claims.text: The stability concerns studied in diffusion sampling relate to DiffSDA's use of efficient diffusion samplers.
claims.verbatim_quote: We incorporate a latent diffusion module (LDM) into our architecture, which enables robust handling of high-dimensional, real-world data

claims.text: Both DNO and DiffSDA use diffusion models to manipulate latent representations — DNO for motion priors, DiffSDA for sequential disentanglement.
claims.verbatim_quote: DiffSDA is modal-agnostic, allowing it to disentangle data across diverse modalities, such as video, audio, and time series

claims.text: Both iterative inpainting and DNO use iterative optimization over diffusion latents — inpainting over noise maps, DNO over noise vectors.
claims.verbatim_quote: we propose a simple algorithm to automatically locate a good noise neighborhood by sample averaging

claims.text: GraphRAG constructs a knowledge graph from source documents as the foundation for query-focused summarization.

claims.verbatim_quote: We use the Leiden algorithm to partition the graph into communities at multiple levels of granularity

claims.text: The agentic graphrag guide builds on GraphRAG's approach of using knowledge graphs for enhanced retrieval and reasoning.
claims.verbatim_quote: The book's strong claim is that harness design, not model capability, sets the ceiling on what an agentic system can do

claims.text: GRAND formulates message passing in GNNs as a continuous diffusion process, generalizing standard discrete GNNs.
claims.verbatim_quote: GRAND approaches deep learning on graphs as a continuous diffusion process, with standard GNNs corresponding to a single-step explicit Euler discretization

claims.text: GLASS Flows builds on the diffusion ODE framework to sample Markov transitions using inner flow matching.
claims.verbatim_quote: GLASS Flows offers a drop-in replacement for ODE and SDE time-reversal in pre-trained diffusion models

claims.text: Both ensemble distillation and Neon are post-training techniques that improve model performance without additional real data.

claims.text: MoE surpasses dense LLMs under equal compute, data, and parameter constraints, demonstrating sparse efficiency.
claims.verbatim_quote: MoE models consistently outperform their dense counterparts under strictly equal resource comparisons

claims.text: UID unifies data-free distillation methods while ensemble distillation compresses multiple models — both are distillation paradigms.
claims.verbatim_quote: UID encompasses SID, FGM, and IBMD as special cases under a unified min-max framework

claims.text: LST addresses modality imbalance through patching while MoE addresses capacity efficiency through sparse activation.
claims.verbatim_quote: Speech tokens require up to three orders of magnitude more tokens than text for equivalent semantic content

claims.text: Both HSD and HB momentum improve inference efficiency — HSD via lossless draft verification, HB via stable numerical integration.
claims.verbatim_quote: HSD is a provably lossless verification method that maximizes the expected number of accepted tokens

claims.text: The crop tool demonstrates the agentic tool-use pattern where Claude iteratively calls tools to analyze images.
claims.verbatim_quote: The crop tool lets Claude zoom in by cropping image regions and re-examining them for fine-grained analysis

claims.text: ACNs demonstrate up to 18% reduction in catastrophic forgetting and 30-80% architectural compression while maintaining accuracy across vision transformers, MLP-mixers, and BERT architectures.
claims.verbatim_quote: Our results demonstrate up to 18% reduction in catastrophic forgetting and 30-80% architectural compression while maintaining accuracy across vision transformers, MLP-mixers, and BERT architectures.

claims.text: AC-ViT converges at 700 epochs on ImageNet-1K, reaching top performance at only 6 layers while vanilla ViT needs all 12 layers.
claims.verbatim_quote: AC-ViT reaches top performance at only 6 layers while the vanilla ViT needs all 12 layers to reach similar performance, effectively suggesting that ACNs can improve inference time and memory consumption without sacrificing performance.

claims.text: ACNs showcase a unique property we coin as auto-compression—the ability of a network to organically compress information during training with gradient descent, through architectural design alone.
claims.verbatim_quote: ACNs showcase a unique property we coin as auto-compression—the ability of a network to organically compress information during training with gradient descent, through architectural design alone, dynamically pushing information to bottom layers, enhancing their representational quality, and naturally revealing redundant in deeper layers.

claims.text: In ACNs the output of each layer is directly connected to the output of the network, and thus is directly optimized by the objective function during gradient descent training.
claims.verbatim_quote: In ACNs the output of each layer is directly connected to the output of the network, and thus is directly optimized by the objective function during gradient descent training.

claims.text: AC-ViT is the auto-compressing variant of Vision Transformer.
claims.verbatim_quote: We train a Vision Transformer (ViT) with long connections (AC-ViT) from scratch on the ILSVRC-2012 ImageNet-1K

claims.text: Due to resource constraints, evaluation of ACNs was limited to relatively small-scale tasks.
claims.verbatim_quote: Due to resource constraints, our evaluation of ACNs was limited to relatively small-scale tasks, though the architecture consistently performed well across modalities, datasets, and state-of-the-art baselines.

claims.text: A notable limitation is the increased training time compared to residual architectures.
claims.verbatim_quote: A notable limitation is the increased training time compared to residual architectures. While this may contribute to stronger representations, optimizing training efficiency remains an open challenge, warranting further exploration of scheduling and initialization strategies.

claims.text: The Direct Gradient (DG) component directly connects from the output to each layer, acting as an information super highway especially early in training.
claims.verbatim_quote: The Backward component (Full Gradient - FG) can be further decomposed into a Network-mediated Gradient (NG) component that is scaled by network weights and backpropagates information through (a subset of) the network and a Direct Gradient (DG) component that directly connects from the output to each layer

claims.text: ACNs utilize a strong DG component coupled with a weaker feed-forward signal to achieve implicit layer-wise training.
claims.verbatim_quote: a strong DG component coupled with a weaker feed-forward signal leads implicitly to efficient layer-wise training

claims.text: Broader validation including large-scale, self-supervised, and multi-task settings is essential to fully understand ACN capabilities and boundaries.
claims.verbatim_quote: Broader validation—including large-scale, self-supervised, and multi-task settings (e.g., language or multimodal models)—is essential to fully understand its capabilities and boundaries.

claims.text: ACNs achieve better average accuracy across all tasks on the Split CIFAR-100 continual learning benchmark, confirming they are a more suitable architecture for continual learning.
claims.verbatim_quote: ACNs consistently exhibit significantly less forgetting (up to 18% improvement) compared to Residual Networks.

claims.text: AC-BERT achieves on-par performance with residual BERT on GLUE tasks while using approximately 75% fewer layers.
claims.verbatim_quote: it naturally converges to using significantly fewer layers (approximately 75% less layers) while maintaining performance comparable to the full residual network.

claims.text: AC-BERT is the auto-compressing variant of BERT architecture for language modeling.
claims.verbatim_quote: We compare the ACN and residual architectures in the standard BERT pre-training and fine-tuning paradigm.

claims.text: ACNs with Synaptic Intelligence show increasing depth decreases forgetting, ideal for CL systems.
claims.verbatim_quote: with SI, increasing ACN depth decreases forgetting—an ideal behavior for CL systems where increasing network capacity reduces forgetting—while Residual Networks show the opposite pattern, indicating potential overfitting.

claims.text: Unlike the symmetric forward and backward terms of ResNets and FFNs, ACNs gradients consist of a single forward path and multiple backward paths.
claims.verbatim_quote: Unlike the symmetric forward and backward terms of ResNets and FFNs, ACNs gradients, as argued, consist of a single forward path and multiple backward paths.

claims.text: ACNs with only the DG component under-perform, underpinning the importance of the NG component for maximizing performance.
claims.verbatim_quote: ACNs with only the DG component under-perform, underpinning the importance of the NG component for maximizing performance.

claims.text: The conditional velocity field is defined as u^cond(x, z=x_1, t) = (x_1 - x)/(1 - t)
The generalization-through-variance hypothesis states that the noisy nature of the conditional flow matching loss drives generalization
claims.verbatim_quote: One recent hypothesis is that generalization arises from the fact that the regression target u^{cond} of conditional flow matching is only a stochastic estimate of hat{u}^*

claims.text: The learned velocity field fails to approximate the optimal velocity at two specific time intervals: around t=0.15 and near t=1
claims.verbatim_quote: the approximation fails at two specific time intervals: around t ≈ 0.15 and near t=1

claims.text: The EFM Monte Carlo estimator has lower conditional variance than the standard conditional flow matching target
EFM consistently yields modest but steady improvements as stochasticity is reduced on CIFAR-10 and CelebA
claims.verbatim_quote: we consistently observe modest but steady improvements as stochasticity is reduced

claims.text: EFM with more samples M outperforms OT-CFM on both CIFAR-10 and CelebA in terms of FID
claims.verbatim_quote: Figure 4 compares vanilla flow matching, OT-CFM, and the empirical flow matching (EFM) approaches

claims.text: The average velocity field u is defined as displacement divided by time interval and relates to instantaneous velocity v via the MeanFlow Identity.
claims.verbatim_quote: We define average velocity as the displacement between two time steps t and r (obtained by integration) divided by the time interval.

claims.text: The MeanFlow Identity u = v - (t-r) d/dt u is the core theoretical contribution that enables training without explicit integral computation.
claims.verbatim_quote: We refer to this equation as the 'MeanFlow Identity', which describes the relation between v and u.

claims.text: MeanFlow provides a principled framework for one-step generative modeling based on average velocity fields.
claims.verbatim_quote: We propose a principled and effective framework, termed MeanFlow, for one-step generation.

claims.text: MeanFlow achieves an FID of 3.43 with 1-NFE on ImageNet 256x256, significantly outperforming prior methods.
claims.verbatim_quote: our method achieves an FID of 3.43 using 1-NFE (Number of Function Evaluations) generation.

claims.text: MeanFlow achieves nearly 70% relative improvement vs previous SOTA Shortcut models for 1-NFE generation.
claims.verbatim_quote: MeanFlow has nearly 70% relative improvement vs. the previous state-of-the-art (10.60, Shortcut [13]).

claims.text: MeanFlow achieves 3.43 FID vs IMM's 7.77 for one-step generation, a 50%+ relative improvement.
claims.verbatim_quote: it achieves 3.43 FID, which is an over 50% relative improvement vs. IMM's one-step result of 7.77

claims.text: MeanFlow with 2-NFE achieves FID 2.20, on par with DiT (FID 2.27) at 250-NFE under same XL/2 backbone.
claims.verbatim_quote: This result is on par with the leading baselines of many-step diffusion/flow models, namely, DiT (FID 2.27)

claims.text: MeanFlow incorporates CFG into ground-truth fields, enabling single-NFE sampling with guidance.
claims.verbatim_quote: Our method naturally supports classifier-free guidance (CFG). Rather than naively applying CFG at sampling time, which would double NFE, we treat CFG as a property of the underlying ground-truth fields.

claims.text: MeanFlow uses JVP to compute the total derivative d/dt u in the MeanFlow Identity, requiring only a single extra backward pass.
claims.verbatim_quote: computing d/dt u via jvp requires only a single backward pass, similar to standard backpropagation in neural networks.

claims.text: MeanFlow achieves competitive FID of 2.92 on unconditional CIFAR-10 with 1-NFE, without EDM preconditioner.
claims.verbatim_quote: On this dataset, our method is competitive with prior approaches.

claims.text: Unlike Consistency Models that rely on consistency constraints anchored at data side, MeanFlow is driven by the definitional identity of average velocity.
claims.verbatim_quote: Consistency Models [46, 43, 15, 31] are focused on paths anchored at the data side: in our notations, this corresponds to fixing r=0 for any t.

claims.text: Incorrect JVP tangent vectors severely degrade performance, with correct (v,0,1) achieving FID 61.06.
claims.verbatim_quote: It shows that meaningful results are achieved only when the JVP computation is correct.

claims.text: MeanFlow exhibits promising scalability with larger model sizes and longer training for 1-NFE generation.
claims.verbatim_quote: MeanFlow models exhibit promising scalability for 1-NFE generation.

claims.text: Orthogonal improvements like REPA are applicable to MeanFlow and left for future work.
claims.verbatim_quote: Orthogonal improvements, such as REPA [51], are applicable, which are left for future work.

claims.text: Flow Matching provides framework for flow paths but requires iterative multi-step ODE solving for generation.
claims.verbatim_quote: Flow Matching [28, 2, 30] provides an intuitive and conceptually simple framework for constructing flow paths that transport one distribution to another.

claims.text: MeanFlow builds on the Flow Matching framework, modifying the matching target with a JVP-derived correction term.
claims.verbatim_quote: our method is conceptually simple: it behaves similarly to Flow Matching, with the key difference that the matching target is modified by -(t-r)(v_t partial_z u_theta + partial_t u_theta)

claims.text: MeanFlow is evaluated on ImageNet 256x256 using pre-trained VAE latent space.
claims.verbatim_quote: We conduct our major experiments on ImageNet [7] generation at 256x256 resolution.

claims.text: TLI (Algorithm 4) employs token-level rejection sampling verification, adjusting the drafter's distribution to sample only from the vocabulary intersection.
claims.verbatim_quote: A purely token-based approach that adjusts the drafter's distribution to sample only from the intersection between the two vocabularies and employs the standard SD verification method.

claims.text: SLEM and TLI were integrated into Hugging Face Transformers, becoming the default inference pipeline behavior for heterogeneous SD.
claims.verbatim_quote: Algorithm 2 and Algorithm 4 had become the default inference pipeline behavior (in October 2024 and February 2025, respectively), enabling efficient speculative decoding (SD) for heterogeneous vocabularies across diverse applications.

claims.text: SLEM demonstrates throughput accelerations of up to 2.8x over autoregressive decoding on summarization, programming, and long-context tasks.
claims.verbatim_quote: our algorithms demonstrate significant speedups of up to 2.8x over standard autoregressive decoding

claims.text: TLI demonstrates speedups of up to 1.7x over autoregressive decoding.
claims.verbatim_quote: Empirically, Algorithm 4 demonstrates significant speedups of up to 1.7x over autoregressive decoding.

claims.text: Algorithm 3 (SLRS) is lossless, meaning outputs preserve the target distribution, proven in Theorem 3.2.
claims.verbatim_quote: Algorithm 3 is lossless, as Theorem 3.2 proves, because it uses the same structure as the standard verification method of SD, which is lossless

claims.text: SLRS guarantees higher expected acceptance rates than SLEM under the same target distribution, as proven in Theorem 3.1.
claims.verbatim_quote: We prove that it is lossless (Theorem 3.2) and guarantees higher expected acceptance rates than string-level exact matching, under the same target distribution (Theorem 3.1).

claims.text: Non-injective tokenizers significantly impact acceptance rates of SLEM; Algorithm 2 handles this through suffix/prefix matching.
claims.verbatim_quote: A common issue with tokenizers is that they do not always implement an injective function, meaning that for any given string s, it is possible for s != decode(encode(s)).

claims.text: Calculating psi(t) for SLRS grows exponentially with target token length, making it computationally expensive for practical use with larger vocabularies.
claims.verbatim_quote: calculating psi(t) during runtime might be too computationally expensive for practical use cases—especially if the drafter's vocabulary includes long tokens

claims.text: SLRS uses a lookahead indicator function to control the tradeoff between acceptance probability and number of drafter forward passes.
claims.verbatim_quote: The lookahead controls a tradeoff between the probability of accepting a token and the number of drafter forwards, since every sampling of a draft token requires computing a forward pass of the drafter.

claims.text: The applicability of SLRS to a wider range of drafters with larger vocabularies is an open question requiring further research.
claims.verbatim_quote: the applicability of Algorithm 3 to a wider range of drafters with larger vocabularies is an open question that requires further research, and we propose it as future work.

claims.text: When the vocabulary intersection is empty, the acceptance rate of TLI is zero, a known limitation of SD algorithms.
claims.verbatim_quote: if the intersection is empty, the acceptance rate of both algorithms is zero. This dependency on acceptance rate is not new or unique. Instead, it is a known limitation of SD algorithms.

claims.text: MeanFlow's CFG formulation enables single-NFE sampling with guidance, unlike naive CFG that doubles NFE.
claims.verbatim_quote: This formulation allows us to enjoy the benefits of CFG while maintaining the 1-NFE behavior during sampling.

claims.text: The paper challenges the belief that target stochasticity drives generalization in flow matching
claims.verbatim_quote: We challenge the prevailing belief that generalization in flow matching stems from an inherently noisy loss (Section 3.1). This assumption, largely supported by studies in low-dimensional settings, fails to hold in realistic high-dimensional data regimes.

claims.text: Generalization emerges when the limited-capacity neural network fails to approximate the optimal closed-form velocity field
claims.verbatim_quote: we observe that generalization in flow matching emerges precisely when the limited-capacity neural network fails to approximate the optimal closed-form velocity field (Section 3.2).

claims.text: On the 2D two-moons dataset the transition between stochastic and deterministic velocity occurs at intermediate-to-large t values
claims.verbatim_quote: On the two-moons toy dataset, this transition occurs for intermediate-to-large values of t

claims.text: On high-dimensional CIFAR-10, non-stochasticity arises very early before t=0.2, meaning the non-stochastic regime dominates most of the generative process
claims.verbatim_quote: for real, high-dimensional data, non-stochasticity arises very early (before t=0.2 for CIFAR-10)

claims.text: As dimensionality increases through varying image resolutions of Imagenette, the alignment between optimal and conditional velocity converges to 100%
claims.verbatim_quote: as the dimensionality increases, the proportion of high-cosine matches rapidly converges to 100%

claims.text: Algorithm 2 (SLEM) addresses heterogeneous vocabularies by using plain text as a shared intermediate representation between draft and target vocabularies.
claims.verbatim_quote: Algorithm 2 is one solution to the problem of heterogeneous vocabularies. It implements a variant of SD with the verification method of exact matching. The key mechanism involves translating tokens bidirectionally between the draft and target vocabularies.

claims.text: All three proposed algorithms remove the shared-vocabulary constraint of standard SD, allowing off-the-shelf models to serve as drafters without retraining.
claims.verbatim_quote: We relax a key constraint of the speculative decoding (SD) framework—the requirement that the drafter must use the same vocabulary as the target model. By allowing heterogeneous vocabularies, we eliminate the requirement to train a drafter from scratch and enable any model to operate as drafter

claims.text: Algorithm 2 supports non-injective tokenizers by searching for the longest stretch of matched tokens between accepted and newly proposed sequences.
claims.verbatim_quote: To address this issue and broaden the applicability of Algorithm 2 to a wider range of tokenizers, we propose the following simple solution.

claims.text: Loss metric power p=1 achieves best FID of 61.06 in ablation, while p=0 (squared L2) underperforms.
claims.verbatim_quote: Tab. 1e shows that p = 1 achieves the best result

claims.text: Generalization in flow matching refers to the ability to generate novel samples beyond the training data
claims.verbatim_quote: How can flow matching generalize if the optimal velocity field only generates training samples?

claims.text: Generalization occurs predominantly at small time values along flow matching trajectories
claims.verbatim_quote: generalization occurs at small times

claims.text: Following the optimal velocity until tau >= 0.4 yields generated samples almost identical to training data, meaning generalization fails
claims.verbatim_quote: as soon as τ ≥ 0.4, the sample generated by the hybrid model is almost the same as the one obtained with û^* (τ = 1)

claims.text: Explicitly regressing against the optimal closed-form velocity field does not impair generalization and can enhance it
claims.verbatim_quote: we show that explicitly regressing against the optimal closed-form velocity field does not impair generalization and can, in some cases, enhance it (Section 4).

claims.text: Exactly minimizing the conditional flow matching loss would result in memorization because the optimal velocity field only generates training samples
claims.verbatim_quote: exactly minimizing the conditional flow matching loss would result in u_θ = û^*, meaning the model memorizes the training data and fails to generalize

claims.text: Locality and equivariance induce combinatorial creativity by preventing optimal score-matching
claims.verbatim_quote: We identify two simple inductive biases, locality and equivariance, that: (1) induce a form of combinatorial creativity by preventing optimal score-matching

claims.text: ELS predicts CNN outputs with median r^2 of 0.95 on CIFAR10
claims.verbatim_quote: after calibrating a single time-dependent hyperparameter can quantitatively predict the outputs of trained convolution only diffusion models (like ResNets and UNets) with high accuracy (median r^2 of 0.95, 0.94, 0.94, 0.96 for our top model on CIFAR10, FashionMNIST, MNIST, and CelebA)

claims.text: Ideal score machine can only memorize training data
claims.verbatim_quote: any diffusion model that learns the true score s_t on a finite dataset D must memorize the training data and cannot creatively generate new samples far from the training data

claims.text: ELS creates novel images through locally consistent patch mosaics
claims.verbatim_quote: samples generated from these machines are locally consistent in the sense that they obeying 3 local conditions

claims.text: Locality scale changes from large to small over time in reverse flow
claims.verbatim_quote: it changes from large to small scales as time passes from early (large t) to late (small t) in the reverse flow

claims.text: Theory reproduces spatial inconsistencies via excess late-time locality
claims.verbatim_quote: our theory, since it matches trained model outputs on a case by case basis, also reproduces these inconsistencies

claims.text: ELS partially predicts UNet+SA outputs with median r^2 ~ 0.77 on CIFAR10
claims.verbatim_quote: our ELS model, with no modification whatsoever, predicts the UNet+SA outputs on a case-by-case basis with a median of r^2 ~ 0.77 on 100 sample images

claims.text: Omega-local score depends only on local neighborhood Omega_x
claims.verbatim_quote: M_t[phi] is defined to be Omega-local if, for all images phi and all pixel locations x, M_t[phi](x) depends on phi only through phi_{Omega_x}

claims.text: G-equivariant model satisfies M_t[Uphi] = UM_t[phi]
claims.verbatim_quote: A model M_t is defined to be G-equivariant with respect to the action of a group G on data if for any U in G, M_t satisfies M_t[Uphi] = UM_t[phi]

claims.text: ELS allows patches from any location, LS limited to same location
claims.verbatim_quote: Further constraining the LS machine with equivariance leads to the ELS machine in which any local image patch at any pixel location x can now flow towards any local training set image patch drawn from any location x' not necessarily equal to x

claims.text: ELS achieves exponential creativity by mixing local training patches
claims.verbatim_quote: The ELS machine can creatively generate exponentially many novel images by mixing and matching local training set patches and placing them at any location in the generated image

claims.text: For LS/ELS/boundary-broken ELS: phi_0(x) = central pixel of closest local training patch
claims.verbatim_quote: For the LS, ELS, and boundary broken ELS machines, assuming lim_{t->0} phi_t and lim_{t->0} partial_t phi_t exist, then for every pixel x, phi_0(x) = varphi(0) for the unique patch varphi in P_Omega^x(D) for which phi_{Omega_x} is closer in L2 distance

claims.text: LS better explains UNet on CelebA (r^2 ~ 0.90) than ELS
claims.verbatim_quote: unlike on other datasets, that the UNet behavior is more accurately described by the local score machine rather than the ELS machine on CelebA32x32

claims.text: DDIM deterministic parameterization used in this paper
claims.verbatim_quote: Denoising diffusion implicit models (DDIMs), an alternative deterministic parameterization which we primarily use in this paper

claims.text: ELS with ResNet achieves median r^2 ~ 0.96 on CelebA32x32
claims.verbatim_quote: the ELS machine (median r^2 ~ 0.96)

claims.text: Image boundaries anchor generation by providing location info
claims.verbatim_quote: indicating the fundamental importance of boundaries in anchoring diffusion models

claims.text: MoLE restricts inputs to routed experts to embedding tokens, allowing re-parameterization into computation-free LUTs before inference, unlike MoE which must load expert parameters into VRAM.
claims.verbatim_quote: MoLE restricts the input to experts to a limited set (embedding tokens), allowing the experts to be re-parameterized into LUTs before inference, thus avoiding the need to load expert parameters.

claims.text: MoLE is a fully differentiable model, avoiding router collapse and training instability without needing auxiliary losses.
claims.verbatim_quote: Unlike MoE, MoLE is a fully differentiable model, so during training, we do not encounter issues like router collapse or instability. Therefore, we do not use any additional auxiliary losses.

claims.text: MoLE re-parameterizes FFN experts into LUTs before inference, where LUT outputs are retrieved based on input ids.
claims.verbatim_quote: the experts are reparameterized from FFNs into LUTs, eliminating the need for any computation. Each expert only requires a single lookup operation.

claims.text: Existing MoE expert offloading suffers from high latency: loading experts for a single step requires up to 11.3B parameter transfer, incurring 0.7s latency on PCIe 4.0x16.
claims.verbatim_quote: each expert is 176M in size, and loading the necessary experts for a single decoding step would require up to 11.3B of parameter transfer. If offloading to CPU VRAM is selected, using a GPU with PCIe 4.0x16 would still incur a transfer latency of 0.7s per step.

claims.text: Quantizing LUTs to NF4 reduces storage to 25.3% of original size with minimal performance loss, indicating significant redundancy in LUTs.
claims.verbatim_quote: the model's performance suffers minimal loss, while the storage burden and size of transferred data are reduced to 25.3% and 19.5% of the original size, respectively. This indicates that the LUTs still contains significant redundancy and has the potential for further compression.

claims.text: MoLE activates all experts simultaneously rather than sparsely, avoiding router collapse and supporting batched generation without additional VRAM overhead.
claims.verbatim_quote: MoLE allows all experts to be activated simultaneously.

claims.text: The per-token loaded parameter count in MoLE is dN versus 2dkDr in MoE, making MoLE hundreds to thousands of times more efficient.
claims.verbatim_quote: the number of parameters loaded per token in MoLE will be only a fraction — often hundreds or even thousands of times smaller — compared to the number of parameters loaded per token in MoE.

claims.text: UNet creates more structured faces on CelebA, better explained by LS than ELS
claims.verbatim_quote: the UNet creates more structured faces than the ResNet

claims.text: Attention layers carve semantically coherent objects from ELS patch mosaics
claims.verbatim_quote: the UNet+SA appears to carve a semantically coherent object out of the patch mosaic of the ELS machine

claims.text: MoLE eliminates the need to load expert parameters during inference by replacing expert FFNs with pre-computed lookup tables.
claims.verbatim_quote: the experts can be reparameterized as lookup tables (LUTs) that retrieves expert outputs based on input ids, and offloaded to storage devices. Therefore, we do not need to perform expert computations during inference.

claims.text: LUTs in MoLE are 2.4 to 7.4 times larger than the offloaded experts in MoE, but this is acceptable for scalable storage devices.
claims.verbatim_quote: the size of the LUTs in MoLE is 2.4 to 7.4 times larger than the size of the offloaded experts in MoE. However, since these parameters are offloaded to large, scalable storage devices, we believe the storage overhead of the LUTs remains within an acceptable range.

claims.text: Grafting is a simple two-stage approach for editing pretrained DiTs to materialize new architectures under small compute budgets.
claims.verbatim_quote: we present grafting, a simple approach for editing pretrained diffusion transformers (DiTs) to materialize new architectures under small compute budgets.

claims.text: Stage 1 of grafting performs activation distillation to initialize new operators by regressing to the original operator's outputs.
claims.verbatim_quote: This stage transfers the functionality of the original operator to the new one by distilling its activations using a regression objective.

claims.text: Stage 2 of grafting performs lightweight finetuning with limited data to mitigate error propagation from integrating multiple new operators.
claims.verbatim_quote: This stage mitigates error propagation caused by integrating multiple new operators by finetuning using limited data.

claims.text: Full self-grafting with 10% training data achieves near-baseline FID of 2.49 for MHA and 2.54 for MLP vs DiT-XL/2 baseline of 2.27.
claims.verbatim_quote: Using just 10% of the training data (128k samples), we achieve an FID of 2.53 vs. 2.27 for the baseline.

claims.text: Many interleaved hybrid architecture designs achieve FID scores between 2.38 and 2.64, within 0.5 of the DiT-XL/2 baseline.
claims.verbatim_quote: several interleaved hybrid architecture designs achieve FID scores between 2.38 and 2.64 (DiT-XL/2 256x256 baseline: 2.27), showing that grafting can construct good quality hybrids

claims.text: Grafting PixArt-Sigma with Hyena-X achieves 1.43x speedup with less than 2% drop in GenEval score (47.78 vs 49.75).
claims.verbatim_quote: our grafted model achieves a 1.43x speedup in wall-clock time, with a small drop in GenEval score (47.78 vs. 49.75).

claims.text: Grafting enables rewiring sequential transformer blocks into parallel, reducing model depth 2x (28 to 14) with 6% parameter increase.
claims.verbatim_quote: we rewire DiT-XL/2 by parallelizing every pair of transformer blocks, as modern GPUs favor parallel over sequential computation. This reduces model depth by 2x

claims.text: Hyena-X is a new efficient gated convolution operator designed as a drop-in MHA replacement with linear sequence length scaling.
claims.verbatim_quote: We introduce Hyena-X and Hyena-Y two new efficient gated convolution operators designed as drop-in replacements for MHA.

claims.text: Band-k metric quantifies MHA locality: at k=32, 15 out of 28 layers in DiT-XL/2 exhibit over 50% attention mass within the band.
claims.verbatim_quote: At k=32, 15 out of 28 layers exhibit values exceeding 0.5, indicating that several MHA operators model local interactions.

claims.text: For MHA operator distillation, L1 regression achieves best FID (2.51) followed by Huber (2.55) and L2 (2.58).
claims.verbatim_quote: L1 achieves the best FID (2.51), followed by Huber (2.55) and L2 (2.58).

claims.text: For MLP operator distillation L2 performs best (FID 2.33), while L1 underperforms (2.83), due to MLP having 2x more parameters.
claims.verbatim_quote: For MLPs, L2 performs best (2.33), while L1 underperforms (2.83); notably, MLPs have 2x more parameters than MHA which explains its robustness to outliers

claims.text: Interleaved replacement strategy yields best FID (2.74), outperforming top-local (3.02), low-local (3.18), and deep (4.00) heuristics.
claims.verbatim_quote: Interleaved yields the best FID (2.74), followed by top-local (3.02), low-local (3.18), and deep (4.00).

claims.text: SWA remains effective at 75% interleaved MHA replacement with FID 3.09, showing surprising effectiveness of local operators.
claims.verbatim_quote: SWA remains effective at 75% interleaved replacement (FID=3.09).

claims.text: Grafting primarily focuses on DiT architecture editing; generalizing to other model families like autoregressive models remains future work.
claims.verbatim_quote: Our experiments primarily focus on DiTs, and generalizing grafting to other model families, such as autoregressive models, is a direction for future research.

claims.text: For high-resolution PixArt-Sigma grafting, LoRA (rank=64) is used for finetuning to manage memory demands of 16,384-token sequences.
claims.verbatim_quote: We use LoRA (rank=64) [29] for finetuning. LoRA enables efficient finetuning by managing the high memory demands associated with long sequences (16,384 tokens).

claims.text: The framework uses ACT-PAE to encode variable-length motion segments into a unified phase manifold
claims.verbatim_quote: ACT-PAE creates a motion latent manifold that captures important semantic and transition-aware phase information for each motion segment

claims.text: The framework uses SPDM to denoise phase parameters by incorporating semantic instructions via CLIP text embeddings
claims.verbatim_quote: SPDM is designed to denoise phase parameters so that the corresponding decoded motion segment is aligned to the semantic condition

claims.text: The framework uses TPDM to denoise phase parameters using phase dynamics from adjacent motions for smooth transitions
claims.verbatim_quote: TDPM is designed to denoise phase parameters such that the resulting decoded motions are transitionally aligned with adjacent motions

claims.text: Motion inbetweening is made possible by keeping the phase parameter of input motion sequences fixed throughout the diffusion process
claims.verbatim_quote: motion inbetweening task is made possible by keeping the phase parameter of the input motion sequences fixed throughout the diffusion process

claims.text: ACT-PAE handles variable-length motion directly using a transformer, unlike PAE's fixed-window convolution scheme
claims.verbatim_quote: Unlike PAE, which processes motion using a convolution scheme and derives phase parameters from the FFT results, the ACT-PAE encoder directly processes variable-length motion using a transformer and predicts their phase parameters

claims.text: The framework uses BABEL-TEACH as it provides annotated subsequence pairs essential for learning transitions between motion sequences
claims.verbatim_quote: We use the BABEL-TEACH dataset for training and evaluation, as it provides annotated subsequence pairs essential for long-term motion generation

claims.text: SPDM employs the pre-trained CLIP-ViT-B/32 to encode text conditions into embedding vectors for semantic guidance
claims.verbatim_quote: SPDM employs the pre-trained CLIP-ViT-B/32 to encode the input text conditions into embedding vector C_p

claims.text: The framework uses FID to assess motion realism in generated compositional motion pairs and long-term sequences
claims.verbatim_quote: We assess the results of compositional motion pair generation, long-term motion generation, and conditional motion inbetweening based on Frechet Inception Distance (FID) for Motion Realism and Multimodal Distance (MMD) for Text Alignment

claims.text: The framework excels at generating compositional motion sequences that align semantically with input conditions while preserving phase transitional continuity
claims.verbatim_quote: Experimental results demonstrate the competitive performance of our proposed framework in generating compositional motion sequences that align semantically with the input conditions, while preserving phase transitional continuity between preceding and succeeding motion clips

claims.text: The framework is scalable and can process an arbitrary number of motion segments simultaneously via parallel module execution
claims.verbatim_quote: This framework can process an arbitrary number of motion segments of varying lengths simultaneously by leveraging parallel module execution, ensuring smooth and coherent transitions between clips

claims.text: MGD3 addresses dataset diversity in three stages: Mode Discovery, Mode Guidance, Stop Guidance
claims.verbatim_quote: We propose a mode-guided diffusion model leveraging a pre-trained diffusion model without the need to fine-tune with distillation losses

claims.text: MGD3 eliminates need for fine-tuning diffusion models with distillation losses, reducing computational cost
claims.verbatim_quote: Our method eliminates the need for fine-tuning diffusion models with distillation losses, significantly reducing computational costs

claims.text: MGD3 achieves 4.4% accuracy gain on ImageNette
claims.verbatim_quote: achieving accuracy gains of 4.4%, 2.9%, 1.6%, and 1.6% on ImageNette, ImageIDC, ImageNet-100, and ImageNet-1K, respectively

claims.text: MGD3 achieves 1.6% accuracy gain on ImageNet-100
claims.verbatim_quote: achieving accuracy gains of 4.4%, 2.9%, 1.6%, and 1.6% on ImageNette, ImageIDC, ImageNet-100, and ImageNet-1K

claims.text: MGD3 achieves 1.6% accuracy gain on ImageNet-1K
claims.verbatim_quote: achieving accuracy gains of 4.4%, 2.9%, 1.6%, and 1.6% on ImageNette, ImageIDC, ImageNet-100, and ImageNet-1K

claims.text: Mode discovery estimates N modes of a class using VAE encoder latent space with K-Means clustering
claims.verbatim_quote: In the mode discovery stage, the main objective is to identify the N modes of a specific class in the original dataset distribution

claims.text: Mode guidance score g_t = (m_i - x_hat^0_t) guides reverse process toward specific mode
claims.verbatim_quote: the mode guidance score is computed for a particular mode m^i using the following equation: g_t = (m_i - x_hat^0_t)

claims.text: Stop guidance halts mode guidance at tstop timestep to preserve sample quality
claims.verbatim_quote: maintaining strong guidance towards a particular mode m^i throughout the full reverse process often compromises class fidelity and introduces image artifacts

claims.text: MGD3 uses DiT-XL/2 as pre-trained diffusion backbone with 50 sampling steps and CFG scale 4.0
claims.verbatim_quote: Our pre-trained model G is DiT-XL/2 trained on ImageNet

claims.text: MinMax Diffusion requires fine-tuning with representative and diversity losses, while MGD3 does not
claims.verbatim_quote: MinMax Diffusion addresses this by explicitly fine-tuning the model with representative and diversity losses

claims.text: MGD3 builds on classifier-free guidance with guidance scale w=4.0
claims.verbatim_quote: we use the sampling strategy described in Peebles & Xie (2023), which uses 50 sampling steps using classifier-free guidance with a guidance scale of 4.0

claims.text: Mode guidance uses DDIM for efficient sampling
claims.verbatim_quote: We leverage denoising diffusion implicit models (DDIMs) for efficient sampling

claims.text: MGD3 also evaluated using LDM backbone
claims.verbatim_quote: We incorporate a class-conditioned Latent Diffusion Model (LDM) trained on ImageNet-1k

claims.text: Grafted depth-14 model achieves FID 2.77, surpassing DiT-L/2 (FID 3.73) and U-ViT-L (FID 3.44) trained from scratch.
claims.verbatim_quote: Our 14-layer grafted model achieves an FID of 2.77 surpassing DiT variants trained from scratch with similar or increased depth

claims.text: Grafted PixArt-Sigma shows localized artifacts likely due to LoRA adaptation capacity and low-quality synthetic data, with a small GenEval drop.
claims.verbatim_quote: Some localized artifacts are observed in textured regions likely due to LoRA's adaptation capacity and low-quality synthetic data

claims.text: Grafting achieves near-baseline performance using only 10% of ImageNet-1K training data (128k samples) for finetuning.
claims.verbatim_quote: competitive performance can be recovered using only 10% of the training data, even when replacing all MHA or MLP layers in DiT-XL/2.

claims.text: Compositional motion generation involves creating two sequentially connected motion segments (X_p, X_s) with smooth transitions
claims.verbatim_quote: The compositional motion pair generation task focuses on creating two sequentially connected motion segments, X_p and X_s

claims.text: Long-term generation extends compositional generation by producing much longer continuous motion of hundreds or thousands of segments
claims.verbatim_quote: Long-term motion sequence generation extends beyond short-term compositional motion pair generation by producing much longer continuous motion

claims.text: MDMs must learn to solve an exponentially large number of infilling problems at training time, but can decode tokens in arbitrary order at inference time, unlike ARMs.
claims.verbatim_quote: MDMs trade off complexity at training time with flexibility at inference time. At training time, they must learn to solve an exponentially large number of infilling problems, but at inference time, they can decode tokens in essentially arbitrary order.

claims.text: Adaptive MDM inference using Top probability margin improves Sudoku accuracy from 6.88% to 89.49%, outperforming ARMs with 7x parameters trained with supervised order information.
claims.verbatim_quote: adaptive inference can boost solving accuracy in pretrained MDMs from < 7% to about 90%, even outperforming ARMs with 7x as many parameters and that were explicitly trained via teacher forcing to learn the right order of decoding.

claims.text: On L&O-NAE-SAT with (N,P)=(25,275), adaptive inference achieves 93.76% accuracy versus 78.06% for vanilla inference.
claims.verbatim_quote: Adaptive MDM inference achieves better likelihood matching than vanilla MDM inference.

claims.text: Top probability margin provides a more reliable estimate of uncertainty when multiple values have similar probabilities, outperforming top probability on challenging tasks.
claims.verbatim_quote: Top probability margin strategy will provide a better estimate of the uncertainty of a position, and when there is a single best choice of value then top probability and top probability margin work similarly.

claims.text: MDM loss is an average over all possible token permutations, making MDMs order-agnostic learners that solve exponentially more subproblems than ARMs.
claims.verbatim_quote: MDM loss is a linear combination of the loss for all possible infilling masks. The minimizer of the loss learns to solve every masking problem.

claims.text: For L&O distributions with natural left-to-right order, order-aware training (ARMs) is computationally tractable, but many MDM masking subproblems are provably computationally intractable.
claims.verbatim_quote: for such distributions with fixed order, we show that ARMs can efficiently sample from the distributions but for MDMs, we theoretically and empirically demonstrate that a large portion of masking subproblems can be difficult to learn.

claims.text: For L&O-NAE-SAT, MDMs show imbalanced performance: prediction tasks for latent positions exhibit larger errors compared to observation positions.
claims.verbatim_quote: prediction tasks for latent positions (light region) exhibit larger errors compared to those for observation positions (dark region).

claims.text: Top probability margin strategy obtains substantially higher accuracy (89.49%) compared to Top probability strategy (18.51%) on Sudoku puzzles.
claims.verbatim_quote: Top probability margin obtains higher accuracy (89.49%) than Top probability strategy (18.51%). As mentioned in Section 4.1, this is because Top probability margin strategy more reliably estimates uncertainty when multiple competing values are close in probability at a given position, as is often the case in Sudoku.

claims.text: Adaptive MDM inference strategies consistently outperform vanilla MDM inference on LLaDA 8B across coding and math benchmarks.
claims.verbatim_quote: both adaptive MDM inference strategies, top probability and top probability margin, consistently outperform vanilla MDM inference.

claims.text: Vanilla MDM inference inevitably encounters difficult subproblems at inference time due to random token unmasking order, while adaptive inference avoids them.
claims.verbatim_quote: during vanilla MDM inference, MDM inevitably encounters such difficult subproblems at Step (b).

claims.text: Dataset distillation aims to build smaller synthetic dataset achieving similar test performance
claims.verbatim_quote: the goal of dataset distillation is to build a smaller synthetic dataset S where N_S << N_T

claims.text: MGD3 improves diversity of distilled datasets compared to prior diffusion-based methods
claims.verbatim_quote: Improved diversity and representativeness compared to previous diffusion-based methods

claims.text: The framework outperforms sequential generation methods like MDM for long-term compositional motion by avoiding over-smoothing and abrupt stops
claims.verbatim_quote: these approaches often overlook the intrinsic kinematics of each segment, resulting in artifacts like over-smoothing or abrupt stops in transitions

claims.text: Adaptive inference strategies that carefully select which token to unmask next make it possible to sidestep the hard subproblems from training, without modifying how MDMs are trained.
claims.verbatim_quote: the adaptive strategies makes it possible to sidestep the hard subproblems from training. In particular, we find that even without modifying how MDMs are trained, the resulting models' logits contain enough information to determine the right order in which to unmask.

claims.text: Diffusion models oversample prominent modes, creating redundancies in distilled datasets
claims.verbatim_quote: diffusion models often oversample the most prominent modes, which creates redundancies in the distilled dataset

claims.text: IMM is a stable single-stage training procedure for few-step generative models trained from scratch.
claims.verbatim_quote: We propose Inductive Moment Matching (IMM), a stable, single-stage training procedure that learns generative models from scratch for single- or multi-step inference.

claims.text: IMM learns via induction: p_{s|t}^theta_n matches p_{s|r}^theta_{n-1} for r < t, bootstrapping from accurate near-s predictions to farther steps.
claims.verbatim_quote: we can construct an inductive learning algorithm for p^theta_{s|t} by using samples from p^theta_{s|r}

claims.text: IMM achieves state-of-the-art 2-step FID of 1.98 on CIFAR-10 for a model trained from scratch.
claims.verbatim_quote: IMM achieves state-of-the-art performance of 1.98

claims.text: IMM achieves 1.99 FID on ImageNet-256x256 with only 8 inference steps using standard transformer architectures.
claims.verbatim_quote: IMM surpasses diffusion models on ImageNet-256x256 with 1.99 FID using only 8 inference steps

claims.text: IMM uses DDIM as its self-consistent interpolant, enabling deterministic one-step mappings between marginals.
claims.verbatim_quote: We show in Appendix C.1 that DDIM as an interpolant is self-consistent.

claims.text: CMs with L2 loss are a single-particle, first-moment-only special case of IMM, explaining CM training instability.
claims.verbatim_quote: we prove that Consistency Models (CMs) are a single-particle, first-moment matching special case of IMM, which partially explains the training instability of CMs.

claims.text: Marginal-preserving interpolants ensure q_s(x_s) is the same regardless of starting time t, enabling consistent training targets.
claims.verbatim_quote: A generalized interpolant x_s is marginal-preserving if for all t and s, the following equality holds...

claims.text: Self-consistency implies marginal preservation: if interpolant satisfies the chain rule composition property, it preserves marginals.
claims.verbatim_quote: self-consistency implies marginal preservation (Lemma 5).

claims.text: IMM uses pushforward sampling to generate samples by directly applying f_{s,t}^theta starting from prior noise.
claims.verbatim_quote: A sample x_s can be obtained by directly pushing x_t through f_{s,t}^theta. This can be iterated for an arbitrary number of steps

claims.text: IMM supports classifier-free guidance by reweighting conditional and unconditional model outputs.
claims.verbatim_quote: during inference time, classifier-free guidance with weight w replaces conditional model output... by a reweighted model output

claims.text: Laplace kernels provide better gradient signals than RBF kernels for MMD estimation in IMM training.
claims.verbatim_quote: We find Laplace kernels provide better gradient signals than RBF kernels.

claims.text: GMMN is a special case of IMM when t=1 and r(s,t)=s=0, reducing to the naive GMMN objective.
claims.verbatim_quote: GMMN is a special case of IMM in that when t=1 and r(s,t)=s=0 our loss reduces to naive GMMN objective.

claims.text: IMM achieves strong FID results on both CIFAR-10 (1.98 2-step) and ImageNet-256x256 (1.99 8-step).
claims.verbatim_quote: We present FID results for unconditional CIFAR-10 and class-conditional ImageNet-256x256

claims.text: ConceptAttention generates saliency maps that precisely locate textual concepts within images
claims.verbatim_quote: We introduce CONCEPTATTENTION, a novel method that leverages the expressive power of DiT attention layers to generate high-quality saliency maps that precisely locate textual concepts within images

claims.text: ConceptAttention produces significantly sharper saliency maps than commonly used cross-attention maps
claims.verbatim_quote: performing linear projections in the output space of DiT attention layers yields significantly sharper saliency maps compared to commonly used cross-attention maps

claims.text: ConceptAttention achieves SOTA on ImageNet-Segmentation with 83.07% accuracy (Flux DiT)
claims.verbatim_quote: CONCEPTATTENTION even achieves state-of-the-art performance on zero-shot image segmentation benchmarks, outperforming 15 other zero-shot interpretability methods on the ImageNet-Segmentation dataset

claims.text: ConceptAttention repurposes parameters of multi-modal DiT attention layers without additional training
claims.verbatim_quote: CONCEPTATTENTION is lightweight and requires no additional training, instead it repurposes the existing parameters of DiT attention layers

claims.text: One-directional attention allows concept embeddings to attend to image patches but not impact image tokens
claims.verbatim_quote: We would like to update our concept embeddings so they are compatible with subsequent layers, but also prevent them from impacting the image tokens

claims.text: ConceptAttention primarily uses Flux DiT architecture for experiments
claims.verbatim_quote: For most of our experiments we use the Flux DiT architecture implemented in PyTorch

claims.text: ConceptAttention achieves 87.85% accuracy on PascalVOC (Flux DiT)
claims.verbatim_quote: We replicate our results quantitatively on both Flux and Stable Diffusion 3.5 Turbo

claims.text: Attention output space saliency produces higher-quality maps than cross attention maps
claims.verbatim_quote: the novel discovery that the output vectors of attention operations produce higher-quality saliency maps than cross attentions

claims.text: ConceptAttention generalizes to CogVideoX video generation multi-modal DiT model
claims.verbatim_quote: CONCEPTATTENTION generalizes to the CogVideoX multi-modal DiT video generation model

claims.text: ConceptAttention outperforms DAAM on ImageNet-Segmentation (83.07 vs 78.47 accuracy)
claims.verbatim_quote: We compare CONCEPTATTENTION to DAAM, TextSpan, and other interpretability methods

claims.text: Flux and SD3 leverage multi-modal DiTs trained to parameterize rectified flow models
claims.verbatim_quote: Flux and Stable Diffusion 3 leverage multi-modal DiTs that are trained to parameterize rectified flow models

claims.text: IMM uses MMD as its divergence metric for matching distributions between different time steps.
claims.verbatim_quote: We choose MMD as our objective due to its superior optimization stability and show that this objective learns the correct data distribution.

claims.text: Loss curves from models of varying sizes collapse onto a single universal curve when compute and loss are normalized to unity at end of training
claims.verbatim_quote: loss curves from models of varying sizes collapse onto a single universal curve when training compute and loss are normalized to unity at the end of training

claims.text: With learning rate decay, collapse becomes so tight that differences in normalized curves across models fall below the noise floor of individual loss curves
claims.verbatim_quote: With learning rate decay, the collapse becomes so tight that differences in the normalized curves across models fall below the noise floor of individual loss curves across random seeds, a phenomenon we term supercollapse

claims.text: The paper connects scaling collapse to the power-law structure in typical neural scaling laws
claims.verbatim_quote: We explain these phenomena by connecting collapse to the power-law structure in typical neural scaling laws

claims.text: Scaling collapse is observed across learning rate schedules, datasets, and architectures including transformers trained on next-token prediction
claims.verbatim_quote: We observe supercollapse across learning rate schedules, datasets, and architectures, including transformers trained on next-token prediction

claims.text: Supercollapse appears in the decay phase of all tested learning rate schedules that decay to zero on CIFAR-5M and Lichess
claims.verbatim_quote: Supercollapse appears in the decay phase of all tested learning rate schedules that decay to zero

claims.text: A simple theoretical model of SGD noise dynamics predicts loss curves under various LR schedules and explains why LR decay suppresses variance
claims.verbatim_quote: we analyze a simple theoretical model of the SGD noise dynamics that predicts loss curves under a variety of learning rate schedules remarkably well

claims.text: Collapse occurs precisely when models are trained for compute-optimal horizons, connecting collapse to power-law Pareto frontiers
claims.verbatim_quote: we first show that for loss curves following typical neural scaling laws, collapse occurs precisely when models are trained for constant multiples of their compute-optimal horizons

claims.text: Replacing muP with constant learning rate across model widths breaks the collapse, showing it is a sensitive indicator of good scaling
claims.verbatim_quote: replacing muP with a constant learning rate across widths breaks the collapse

claims.text: The compute-optimal training horizon is estimated as t^*(p) = (p/p_0)^gamma by extracting the Pareto frontier of expected loss vs compute
claims.verbatim_quote: We estimate the compute-optimal training horizon in tokens for a p-parameter model as t^*(p) = (p/p_0)^gamma

claims.text: The collapse phenomenon generalizes to MLPs trained on synthetic regression tasks with power-law Fourier spectrum
claims.verbatim_quote: we train 7-layer MLPs with varying widths from 384 to 2048 on a synthetic regression task

claims.text: Under supercollapse, the collapse deviation Delta is less than the noise floor sigma for a significant fraction of training
claims.verbatim_quote: Delta(x) < sigma(x,p) for x > 1 - delta for some moderate delta as large as 0.5

claims.text: Setting hat{L} = L_0 to subtract the estimated irreducible loss leads to the best collapse
claims.verbatim_quote: Setting hat{L}=L_0 to subtract the estimated irreducible loss that bottlenecks the asymptotic performance, leading to the best collapse

claims.text: Collapse is observed on CIFAR-5M transformer training with normalized loss curves being nearly identical across model sizes
claims.verbatim_quote: the family of normalized loss curves is nearly identical across p, revealing equal rates of relative progress

claims.text: Under constant LR schedules, collapse deviation is comparable to the noise floor, while LR decay produces supercollapse with deviation below the noise floor
claims.verbatim_quote: For a constant learning rate, Delta(x) quickly rises to a level comparable to sigma(x,p)... Supercollapse appears in the decay phase of all tested learning rate schedules that decay to zero

claims.text: Multi-modal DiTs uniquely enable the creation of rich textual concept embeddings
claims.verbatim_quote: something that is uniquely enabled by multi-modal DiT architectures

claims.text: ConceptAttention achieves 83.07% accuracy on ImageNet-Segmentation vs DINO SA 81.97%
claims.verbatim_quote: Table 1: CONCEPTATTENTION outperforms a variety of Diffusion, DINO, and CLIP ViT interpretability methods

claims.text: Raw cross attention maps are limited to tokens in the user's prompt for multi-modal DiTs
claims.verbatim_quote: these have a key limitation in that their vocabulary is limited to the tokens in the user's prompt

claims.text: ASBS extends AS to handle non-memoryless conditions by solving more general SB problems with arbitrary source distributions.
claims.verbatim_quote: ASBS extends AS to handle non-memoryless conditions by solving more general SB problems

claims.text: The memoryless condition restricts source distribution to Dirac delta, precluding common priors like Gaussian or harmonic oscillators.
